<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 41-year-old woman with worsening perineal pain and pustular skin lesions.  
- Clinical exam: extensive erythematous pustular lesions in perineal and perianal regions, rope-like dermal thickening, multiple fistulous openings with purulent and haemoserous discharge.  
- Ultrasound: dermal/hypodermal band-like structures (fistulas/tunnels), dermal collections around hair follicles with hyperechoic hair fragments, some sinus tracts connecting to epidermis, increased vascularity indicating active inflammation.  
- MRI: skin thickening in perineum and labia majora, hypertrophic scarring, deep subcutaneous fistulas/tunnels extending to ischioanal space and perianal region, left adductor muscle involvement suggestive of myositis, post-contrast rim enhancement of tunnels (active inflammation), no fistulous communication with anal canal lumen.  
- After immunomodulatory therapy: significant lesion reduction, resolution of fistulas and myositis except minor residual skin thickening and scarring, minimal enhancement (minor residual inflammation).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Crohn’s disease:  
  Pros: Can cause perianal fistulas and deep abscesses.  
  Cons: Usually there is fistulous communication with anal canal lumen in Crohn's; here none was identified. Also, no mention of gastrointestinal symptoms or other Crohn's signs. Presence of rope-like dermal thickening and multiple fistulous openings typical of hidradenitis suppurativa rather than Crohn's perianal disease.  
- Hidradenitis suppurativa (HS):  
  Pros: Typical clinical presentation of painful, erythematous, pustular lesions with multiple fistulous tracts and sinus formation in perineal/perianal region. Rope-like dermal thickening and multiple fistulas around hair follicles with hair fragments are characteristic. Imaging shows subcutaneous tunnels and sinus tracts, thickened skin, scarring. HS commonly presents with chronic inflammation, scarring, and may involve deep tissues, including muscles (myositis).  
  Cons: Myositis involvement is less common but reported in severe cases.  
- Perianal fistula:  
  Pros: Multiple fistulous openings and sinus tracts.  
  Cons: Usually perianal fistulas communicate with anal canal lumen, which is absent here. Also no mention of gastrointestinal disease.  
- Fournier gangrene:  
  Pros: Can involve perianal/perineal necrotizing fascia and deep tissues.  
  Cons: Acute, severe, rapidly progressive necrotizing fasciitis with systemic toxicity; not described here. No gas formation reported, no acute fulminant infection features.  
- Carbuncles:  
  Pros: Pustular skin lesions can present as carbuncles.  
  Cons: Carbuncles are focal, superficial skin infections often in hair-bearing areas, but they do not produce deep sinus tracts, fistulas, or muscle involvement.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Hidradenitis suppurativa - fits the clinical features (perineal/pustular lesions, rope-like dermal thickening, multiple sinus tracts), imaging features (hair fragments in lesions, subcutaneous fistulas, myositis), chronicity rather than acute severe infection.  
2) Perianal fistula - possible, but lack of lumen communication and extensive skin involvement with hair fragments more typical for HS.  
3) Crohn’s disease - less likely due to lack of internal fistulas or GI symptoms.  
4) Fournier gangrene - no evidence of necrosis or gas or systemic toxicity.  
5) Carbuncles - less likely due to deep involvement and chronic fistulous tracts.  

</analysis><final> Hidradenitis suppurativa </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old male with a history of traumatic brain injury as an infant with subsequent hydrocephalus status post ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt. Shunt revision was done 2 years back. Elevated blood pressure was noted in the outpatient department and referred to emergency.
 Imaging Findings: CT head demonstrated ventriculomegaly (Figure 1a) compared to the baseline CT one year back (Figure 1b).
 
 The shunt series, including anteroposterior radiographs of the skull, neck, chest, and abdomen, showed a left-sided VP shunt coursing along the neck and chest with the peritoneal part coiled within the right upper quadrant. A large globular density was noted in the right upper quadrant overlying the liver shadow. Prior abandoned shunt is also visualised (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The right upper quadrant ultrasound showed a fluid collection displacing the liver posteriorly (Figure 3).
 
 CT abdomen demonstrated a subcapsular collection of fluid with scalloping of the liver parenchyma. The distal shunt coiled and terminated within the cyst (Figures 4a and 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old male presented to the outpatient clinic with swelling over the right greater trochanteric region, which progressively grew after a fall four months before. He had a previous history of neoadjuvant radiotherapy followed by resection of a soft tissue sarcoma in the left thigh seven years before.
 Imaging Findings: On the preoperative MRI performed seven years before (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 2c), an intramuscular lobulated mass was seen in the left tensor fascia lata, extending into the subcutis. The mass showed areas of high signal on T1WI (Figure 2a), which became hypo-intense after fat suppression (Figure 2b). The mass was heterogeneous, predominantly hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 2b) and showed internal areas of heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium contrast (Figure 2c).
 
 The current MR study revealed a thick-walled, well-defined lesion superficial to the right tensor fascia lata, with heterogeneous high signal on T1WI (Figures 3a and 3b) and T2WI (Figures 4a and 4b), persisting after fat suppression. Multiple internal fluid-fluid levels were present. While post-contrast T1WI sequences (Figure 5) showed intralesional heterogeneous high signal, the dynamic subtraction sequence (Figure 6), only showed a thick peripheral enhancing rim. Therefore, the pseudoenhancement seen on the static sequences was due to a haemorrhage-related high native T1WI signal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old female, with a history of anterior cervical diskectomy and fusion, had a CT head and cervical spine in 2007 for unrelated symptoms. Incidentally, imaging identified a heavily calcified lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The patient remained asymptomatic and, therefore, opted for regular follow-up imaging as opposed to surgical management.
 Imaging Findings: The CT brain demonstrated a 2.5 cm (maximal diameter), highly calcified intra-axial lesion with no surrounding oedema or mass effect, within the right cerebellar fossa (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 The MRI demonstrated the same lesion within the right cerebellar hemisphere, measuring 2.5 cm (maximal axial measurement). The lesion abutted the inferior tentorial surface; however, a broad base against it was not exhibited. There was no notable mass effect or surrounding oedema. On T1 (Figure 3) and T2 (Figure 4) weighted images, the lesion displayed a low signal intensity. Additionally, blooming artefacts were observed on gradient imaging (Figure 5).
 
 During the 17-year follow-up period, the patient exhibited no symptoms. Biennially, MRI heads revealed no change in the lesion size or associated mass effect.

   - Cons: This lesion is intra-axial (within brain parenchyma), no dural base described, no mass effect; well-circumscribed meningiomas often have a dural tail and are extra-axial. Intra-axial meningiomas are rare; no dural attachment here.

   - Pros: Cavernomas can calcify; intra-axial lesion; low T1 and T2 signal due to hemosiderin and calcification; blooming artifact consistent with hemosiderin. No edema or mass effect; cavernomas can be stable over long periods; typically 2.5 cm falls within size range.  
   - Cons: Cavernomas usually have a characteristic "popcorn" or "mulberry" appearance with mixed signal due to hemorrhage products; but this lesion is purely calcified with no surrounding edema. However, calcified cavernomas can appear as heavily calcified.  

3) Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis (CAPNON):  
   - Pros: Lesion characterized by calcification, low T1/T2 signal, intra-axial location; can cause calcified mass lesions. CAPNONs are rare, benign, and often calcified.  
   - Cons: Usually extra-axial but can be intra-axial; small number of cases; no mass effect here; but stable over many years. The description of "highly calcified" fits. Nonetheless, the location is within cerebellar parenchyma near tentorium abutment without dural base may still fit.  

4) Oligodendrogliomas:  
   - Pros: These tumors typically calcify and are intra-axial.  
   - Cons: Usually show mass effect and surrounding edema; grow over time; patient remained asymptomatic for 17 years with no lesion growth. Unlikely to be a low-grade lesion without changes over 17 years and no mass effect.  

5) Choroid plexus papilloma:  
   - Pros: Can have calcifications; usually extra-axial in ventricular system.  
   - Cons: Lesion is intraparenchymal in cerebellar hemisphere, not intraventricular; choroid plexus papillomas do not typically appear as intra-axial masses.

3) Calcified meningioma: less likely due to intra-axial location and lack of dural base.  
4) Oligodendroglioma: less likely due to no growth or edema.  
5) Choroid plexus papilloma: ruled out by location.  

Given typical imaging features and lesion stability, the best fit is calcified cavernoma.  
</analysis>  
<final>Calcified cavernoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 78-year-old male patient with a history of alcoholism and smoking was referred to the otorhinolaryngology department for dysphagia. Clinical examination revealed a painless 4 cm thickening lesion of the uvula. Cervical palpation revealed no adenopathy. A biopsy of the lesion was performed.
 Imaging Findings: CT scan revealed the presence of a uvula tumour (Figure 1), a suspicious hepatic lesion and a solid cystic lesion in the body of the pancreas. MRI showed a pancreatic body lesion, measuring 22 mm, with cystic and tissue components, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 2), and a 55 mm heterogeneous hepatic mass, of the segment IV, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 3). There was no dilatation of the pancreatic or biliary ducts. We performed an FDG PET-CT, which demonstrated an intensely hypermetabolic uvula (SUVmax at 9.2), with moderate hypermetabolism of the adenopathies (SUVmax at 2.5) and the liver lesion (SUVmax at 5.4) (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c).

   - Pros: Moderate FDG uptake in liver mass consistent with metastatic lesion from uvula carcinoma, supported by known primary uvula squamous cell carcinoma and presence of adenopathies.

   - Pros: Typically shows hypervascular lesion with enhancement.

5) Pancreatic serous cystadenoma - least likely given clinical context and lack of metabolic activity typical for benign lesions.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old female patient presented at the Emergency Department with severe acute abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. She also referred cessation of flatus and bowel movements for 4 days. Past medical history is unremarkable. On physical exam, the abdomen was distended and tender with no rebound or guarding.
 Imaging Findings: A plain erect abdominal radiograph revealed the presence of an air-distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant consistent with the inverted cecum, and multiple fluid levels (Figure 1).
 
 An abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) was later performed, with coronal and sagittal reconstructions (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, and 5). On CT images, there was a markedly distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant with air-fluid level, measuring a maximum calliper of 7.5 cm, adjacent to a small blind-ending tubular structure compatible with the ileocecal appendix, consistent with the inverted cecum. A clockwise whirl sign composed of spiralled loops of collapsed cecum and ileum, with twisting of central fatty mesentery with enhancing distended vessels, was present (Figures 4a and 4b). The gradual tapering of the obstructed cecum at the site of the whirl resulted in a stenosis, with the appearance of a bird’s beak (Figure 5). There was also dilation of small bowel loops, filled with fluid. There were no signs of bowel perforation, as well as no signs of pneumatosis intestinalis or other signs of vascular compromise.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Our patient is a 64-year-old male admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) due to a prolonged cardiac arrest, who was on mechanical circulatory support with extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). He was COVID-19-negative and presented with an encephalopathic syndrome.
 Imaging Findings: A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed, and the susceptibility-weighted imaging sequence (SWI), revealed multiple small hypointense foci consistent with cerebral microbleeds (CBMs). These lesions were diffusely distributed across the subcortical white matter, splenium of the corpus callosum and posterior fossa, affecting the brainstem (midbrain and pons), as well as both cerebellar hemispheres. The cortex and periventricular regions were spared (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). In addition, we found small watershed infarctions (Figures 2a and 2b).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: Our patient is a 64-year-old male admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) due to a prolonged cardiac arrest, who was on mechanical circulatory support with extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). He was COVID-19-negative and presented with an encephalopathic syndrome.
 Imaging Findings: A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed, and the susceptibility-weighted imaging sequence (SWI), revealed multiple small hypointense foci consistent with cerebral microbleeds (CBMs). These lesions were diffusely distributed across the subcortical white matter, splenium of the corpus callosum and posterior fossa, affecting the brainstem (midbrain and pons), as well as both cerebellar hemispheres. The cortex and periventricular regions were spared. In addition, we found small watershed infarctions.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Critical illness-associate cerebral microbleeds, Hypertensive encephalopathy, Cerebral amyloid angiopathy, Fat-emboli, COVID-19
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 64-year-old male with prolonged cardiac arrest and ECMO support.  
- Encephalopathic state.  
- Brain MRI with SWI shows multiple small hypointense foci consistent with cerebral microbleeds (CBMs).  
- CBMs distribution: diffuse, subcortical white matter, splenium of corpus callosum, posterior fossa, brainstem (midbrain, pons), cerebellar hemispheres.  
- Cortex and periventricular regions spared.  
- Small watershed infarctions present.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons each:  
1) Critical illness-associated cerebral microbleeds  
   Pros: Direct correlation with prolonged ICU stay, ECMO support, and cardiac arrest; typical CBM distribution includes subcortical white matter and corpus callosum; sparing of cortex and periventricular zones is characteristic. Watershed infarctions can co-occur due to hypoxia/ischemia.  
   Cons: Requires clinical context of critical illness which is present.

2) Hypertensive encephalopathy  
   Pros: Can cause microbleeds, especially in deep gray nuclei.  
   Cons: Typical distribution differs; usually involves basal ganglia, thalami, and brainstem; cortex sparing less consistent; no mention of hypertension or typical radiologic findings like edema or PRES; CBMs distribution is more posterior and corpus callosal than in hypertension.

3) Cerebral amyloid angiopathy  
   Pros: Multiple lobar microbleeds often in superficially located lobar regions.  
   Cons: Typically older patients with dementia; microbleeds usually cortical/subcortical but sparing deep structures and brainstem; no mention of amyloid imaging or cognitive decline; watershed infarctions and ECMO are not typical.

4) Fat-emboli  
   Pros: Can have cerebral microbleeds after orthopedic trauma or surgery.  
   Cons: No history of trauma/surgery involving fat embolism; clinical context is prolonged cardiac arrest and ECMO support; microbleeds distribution is less typical; no mention of fat embolism in history or other signs.

5) COVID-19  
   Pros: COVID-19 can produce cerebral microbleeds and watershed infarctions.  
   Cons: Patient is COVID-19-negative; exclude this diagnosis.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Critical illness-associated cerebral microbleeds (best fit due to clinical context, typical distribution, and involvement of white matter and corpus callosum).  
2) Hypertensive encephalopathy (less likely given distribution and lack of hypertension history).  
3) Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (less likely given age, clinical context, and distribution).  
4) Fat-emboli (unlikely due to lack of trauma/surgery).  
5) COVID-19 (excluded as patient is COVID-19-negative).  
</analysis><final> Critical illness-associate cerebral microbleeds </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman presented with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain for 3 days. She was diagnosed with thrombosis involving the left popliteal vein to the common femoral vein with the possibility of a slow-flow vascular malformation of the left popliteal region. She was referred to our institute for further management.
 Imaging Findings: USG (ultrasonogram)
 
 : Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving the left popliteal vein (Figure 3a), superficial and common femoral veins (Figure 3b) with heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels (Figure 3c).
 
 CTLL angiogram (computed tomography lower limb)
 
 : A lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion (Figure 4a) in the distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular plane), extending into the popliteal vein (Figure 4b), superficial femoral, common femoral and common iliac veins (Figure 4c).
 
 CTPA (computed tomography pulmonary angiogram)
 
 : Eccentric filling defect involving the main and right pulmonary arteries (Figure 1a), extending to the right interlobar artery (Figure 1b), right basal trunk (Figure 1c), and segmental branches of the right upper lobe and left basal trunk [1]. Thrombus noted in the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple cavitary consolidations involving bilateral lower lobe segments (Figure 2).
 
 PET-CT (positron emission tomography – computed tomography)
 
 : High-grade metabolic activity in the left popliteal and thigh lesion (Figure 5b) and in the deep venous tumour thrombus (Figure 5a), bilateral pulmonary arteries (Figure 6b), right atrium (Figure 6a) and ventricle.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman presented with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain for 3 days. She was diagnosed with thrombosis involving the left popliteal vein to the common femoral vein with the possibility of a slow-flow vascular malformation of the left popliteal region. She was referred to our institute for further management.
 Imaging Findings: USG (ultrasonogram): Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving the left popliteal vein, superficial and common femoral veins with a heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels. CTLL angiogram (computed tomography lower limb): A lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion in the distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular plane), extending into the popliteal vein, superficial femoral, common femoral and common iliac veins. CTPA (computed tomography pulmonary angiogram): Eccentric filling defect involving the main and right pulmonary arteries, extending to the right interlobar artery, right basal trunk, and segmental branches of the right upper lobe and left basal trunk [1]. Thrombus noted in the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple cavitary consolidations involving bilateral lower lobe segments. PET-CT (positron emission tomography – computed tomography): High-grade metabolic activity in the left popliteal and thigh lesion and in the deep venous tumour thrombus, bilateral pulmonary arteries, right atrium and ventricle.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma, Slow flow vascular malformation, Leiomyosarcoma, Vascular leiomyomatosis, Tumour thrombus, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Young female (20 years) with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain.  
- Known thrombosis from left popliteal vein to common femoral vein and possible slow-flow vascular malformation.  
- USG confirms DVT in left popliteal, superficial, and common femoral veins with a heteroechoic lesion around vessels.  
- CT angiogram reveals a lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion in distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular), extending intravascularly into popliteal, superficial femoral, common femoral, and common iliac veins.  
- Pulmonary emboli present with filling defects in main and right pulmonary arteries and branches, as well as intracardiac thrombus in right atrium and ventricle.  
- PET-CT shows high-grade metabolic activity in the left thigh lesion, deep venous thrombus, pulmonary arteries, and right heart chambers.  
- Multiple cavitary consolidations in bilateral lower lobes (likely pulmonary infarcts due to emboli).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma  
- Pros: Rare primary tumour of intravascular origin; can show high metabolic activity on PET-CT.  
- Cons: Very rare; clinical history and imaging fit other features less well; usually arises in joints/knees not vessels.

2) Slow flow vascular malformation  
- Pros: Initial suspicion from clinical data; can cause thrombosis by stasis.  
- Cons: Lesion is heterogeneously enhancing with high metabolic activity on PET-CT, inconsistent with benign vascular malformations; also shows intravascular tumour extension, not just thrombosis.

3) Leiomyosarcoma  
- Pros: Aggressive malignant tumour; can show heterogeneous enhancement and high PET-CT uptake; can invade vessels and cause tumor thrombus.  
- Cons: Typical leiomyosarcoma arises from smooth muscle of vessels or uterus, rarely primary intravascular without larger extravascular component.

4) Vascular leiomyomatosis  
- Pros: Benign but locally aggressive tumour of smooth muscle in vessel walls; can extend intravascularly; usually follows similar veins; can extend into heart ("intravenous leiomyomatosis").  
- Cons: Usually in women with uterine leiomyomas; rare in young 20-year-old without known leiomyomas; low metabolic activity (benign).

5) Tumour thrombus  
- Pros: Lesion extends intravascularly with high metabolic activity on PET, consistent with tumour thrombus; PET shows high SUV in thrombus and lesions.  
- Cons: Need to identify primary neoplasm; tumour thrombus often secondary to known cancer (e.g., renal cell carcinoma), but here lesion is intrinsic to thigh vessels.

6) Lymphoma  
- Pros: Can cause intravascular disease and hypermetabolism on PET-CT.  
- Cons: Lymphoma typically arises in lymph nodes or extranodal soft tissue, not usually intravascularly involving veins; imaging pattern (heterogeneously enhancing intravascular mass) less typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Tumour thrombus (most consistent with lesion enhancing, intravascular, high metabolic activity, extension into right heart and pulmonary arteries causing emboli)  
2) Leiomyosarcoma (could produce tumour thrombus and aggressive features)  
3) Vascular leiomyomatosis (benign, intravascular smooth muscle, but less likely with high PET SUV and patient age)  
4) Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma (very rare, not classic location)  
5) Lymphoma (less typical intravascular lesion, imaging pattern atypical)  
6) Slow flow vascular malformation (contradicted by PET metabolic activity and lesion characteristics)  

Considering the lesion is intravascular in the limb veins, showing heterogenous enhancement, high PET activity, and extension into cardiac chambers and pulmonary arteries suggest tumour thrombus rather than bland thrombus or benign malformation. The presence of a metabolic lesion intravascularly implies tumour thrombus as primary process. This tumour thrombus appears to originate from the left thigh lesion, which may be a primary vascular tumour (considering leiomyosarcoma or vascular leiomyomatosis but the high SUV supports malignancy). However, the diagnosis "Tumour thrombus" best reflects the pathological process here, with intravascular tumor and metabolic activity in lesion and thrombus.  

<final>  
Tumour thrombus  
</final><|return|>
options: Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma | Slow flow vascular malformation | Leiomyosarcoma | Vascular leiomyomatosis | Tumour thrombus | Lymphoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 26-year-old male with severe haemophilia A presented to the emergency department with a month-long history of haematuria, dysuria, and lower abdominal pain. The patient is an active smoker and reported a weight loss of 20 kg over the last year. The patient admitted recreational use of ketamine.
 Imaging Findings: Initial ultrasound imaging revealed a low bladder volume with symmetrical bladder wall thickening (Figure 1a) and bilateral hydronephrosis.
 
 CT intravenous urography (IVU) demonstrated mucosal enhancement and confirmed symmetrical bladder wall thickening, forming the classic “hourglass bladder deformity” (Figures 1b and 1c). Additionally, ureteral wall thickening, proximal urinary tract dilation, and hydronephrosis were observed (Figure 2).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 26-year-old male with severe haemophilia A presented to the emergency department with a month-long history of haematuria, dysuria, and lower abdominal pain. The patient is an active smoker and reported a weight loss of 20 kg over the last year. The patient admitted recreational use of ketamine.
 Imaging Findings: Initial ultrasound imaging revealed a low bladder volume with symmetrical bladder wall thickening and bilateral hydronephrosis. CT intravenous urography (IVU) demonstrated mucosal enhancement and confirmed symmetrical bladder wall thickening, forming the classic “hourglass bladder deformity”. Additionally, ureteral wall thickening, proximal urinary tract dilation, and hydronephrosis were observed.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Urinary tract infection, Carcinoma in situ, Ketamine bladder, Kidney stones
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient is a 26-year-old male with severe hemophilia A.  
- Presents with one month of hematuria, dysuria, and lower abdominal pain.  
- Notable history includes recreational ketamine use and significant weight loss.  
- Ultrasound shows low bladder volume, symmetrical bladder wall thickening, and bilateral hydronephrosis.  
- CT IVU reveals mucosal enhancement and symmetrical bladder wall thickening with an "hourglass bladder deformity".  
- Also demonstrates ureteral wall thickening, proximal urinary tract dilation, and hydronephrosis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 2 pros/cons each:  

1) Urinary tract infection (UTI):  
- Pros: Hematuria, dysuria, and lower abdominal pain can be consistent with UTI.  
- Cons: Symmetrical severe bladder wall thickening ("hourglass deformity") and ureteral wall thickening are not typical of standard UTI. Ketamine use and characteristic bladder changes argue against simple infection.  

2) Carcinoma in situ:  
- Pros: Hematuria is a classic symptom of CIS; mucosal enhancement can be seen.  
- Cons: CIS is typically focal rather than symmetrical; "hourglass deformity" and symmetrical bladder wall thickening are unusual for CIS. Also, patient age and history are less typical for bladder carcinoma.  

3) Ketamine bladder:  
- Pros: History of ketamine use and classic "hourglass bladder deformity" on imaging strongly support ketamine-induced cystitis. Bilateral hydronephrosis and ureteral wall thickening are known complications. Weight loss aligns with chronic ketamine use.  
- Cons: None significant; findings are pathognomonic.  

4) Kidney stones:  
- Pros: Bilateral hydronephrosis and urinary symptoms could indicate obstruction from stones.  
- Cons: Imaging does not describe calculi or obstructive uropathy due to stones; bladder wall thickening and hourglass deformity are unrelated to stones; mucosal enhancement suggests inflammation rather than obstruction by stones.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Ketamine bladder (pathognomonic hourglass deformity and patient history).  
2) Carcinoma in situ (less likely due to symmetry and typical age).  
3) Urinary tract infection (symmetry and severity argue against).  
4) Kidney stones (no stones identified, symptoms and imaging better explained by ketamine bladder).  
Unique finding: "hourglass bladder deformity" is a classic imaging sign for ketamine-induced cystitis.  

</analysis>  
<final>  
Ketamine bladder  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old patient is referred from the primary care due to epigastric pain radiating to the left flank, accompanied by asthenia and episodes of postprandial nausea, as well as signs of panniculitis in both lower limbs. Blood tests revealed elevated CA 19-9 levels (135.5 U/mL).
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan was performed, revealing a hypodense lesion with cystic appearance in contact with the anteroinferior margin of the pancreatic tail, with areas of higher density inside it, which are suggestive of septations (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 A contrast-enhanced MRI was conducted to further characterise the lesion. It showed a heterogeneous tumour with an atypical cystic appearance, related to higher signal intensity on T1-weighted and lower intensity on T2-weighted images, when compared to free water and the presence of small cystic rounded areas with higher intensity on T2-weighted images (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Some small foci of signal drop on out-of-phase T1-weighted sequences compared with in-phase T1-weighted sequences were observed inside the lesion (Figures 3a and 3b). Additionally, areas of diffusion restriction were noted within the lesion (Figures 3c and 3d) and contrast-enhancing septations (Figure 3e). There was no visible communication with the main pancreatic duct.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old patient is referred from the primary care due to epigastric pain radiating to the left flank, accompanied by asthenia and episodes of postprandial nausea, as well as signs of panniculitis in both lower limbs. Blood tests revealed elevated CA 19-9 levels (135.5 U/mL).
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan was performed, revealing a hypodense lesion with cystic appearance in contact with the anteroinferior margin of the pancreatic tail, with areas of higher density inside it, which are suggestive of septations. A contrast-enhanced MRI was conducted to further characterise the lesion. It showed a heterogeneous tumour with an atypical cystic appearance, related to higher signal intensity on T1-weighted and lower intensity on T2-weighted images, when compared to free water and the presence of small cystic rounded areas with higher intensity on T2-weighted images. Some small foci of signal drop on out-of-phase T1-weighted sequences compared with in-phase T1-weighted sequences were observed inside the lesion. Additionally, areas of diffusion restriction were noted within the lesion and contrast-enhancing septations. There was no visible communication with the main pancreatic duct.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm, Mucinous cystic neoplasm, Lymphoepithelial cysts of the pancreas, Serous cystic neoplasm, Pancreatic pseudocyst, Epidermoid cyst

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 27-year-old male with progressive imbalance while walking and generalised tonic-clonic seizures from 5 years of age. No significant family history. Neurological examination revealed lower limb spasticity, brisk reflexes, extensor plantar reflex, features of ataxia, impaired pain, and touch sensations. Ophthalmic and fundus examinations were normal.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain revealed the atrophy of the superior cerebellar vermis and the atrophy of the superior cerebellar cortex. The inferior vermis and the inferior cerebellar cortex are spared (Figures 2 and 3). T2/FLAIR linear hypointense stripes in the pons at the level of superior and middle cerebellar peduncle giving a tigroid/striped appearance (Figures 1a and 1b). Atrophy of the cervical and thoracic spinal cord is seen (Figure 3). Mildly atrophic pre-central and post-central gyri (Figure 4) with the thinned-out body of corpus callosum (Figure 2) also seen.
PostDescription:

The inferior vermis and the inferior cerebellar cortex are spared. Mildly atrophic pre-central and post-central gyri with the thinned-out body of corpus callosum also seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 44-year-old woman with a history of controlled asthma and splenectomy following a gunshot wound 30 years prior presented with a dry cough, occasionally producing yellow-green sputum. She reported intermittent moderate dyspnoea and wheezing on auscultation, with symptoms gradually worsening over the past 30 days.
 Imaging Findings: Chest computed tomography (CCT) revealed solid nodules with homogeneous attenuation and enhancement (Figure 1), smooth borders, no calcifications (Figure 2), and no distortion of the adjacent lung parenchyma within the left pleural space (Figure 3). The nodules were randomly distributed along the longitudinal axis and measured up to 5.9 cm at the base of the hemithorax (Figure 4). A previous splenectomy was confirmed by the absence of the spleen in the abdominal cavity (Figure 5).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 44-year-old woman with a history of controlled asthma and splenectomy following a gunshot wound 30 years prior presented with a dry cough, occasionally producing yellow-green sputum. The nodules were randomly distributed along the longitudinal axis and measured up to 5.9 cm at the base of the hemithorax. A previous splenectomy was confirmed by the absence of the spleen in the abdominal cavity.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 76-year-old male patient with a prior diagnosis of cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) was referred to the emergency department from a nursing home due to progressive clinical deterioration over the past 24 hours. The patient presented decreased consciousness. No fever or recent trauma was reported. An urgent CT scan was requested (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 4).
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT performed four years earlier at the onset of CAA (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) showed an isolated convexity subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) in the right frontal sulcus without trauma, in a patient with mild cognitive impairment.
 
 In the MRI one month after SAH, susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) sequence (Figure 2), significant superficial haemosiderosis and multiple supra and infratentorial microhaemorrhages are observed as hypointense areas with a “blooming” effect.
 
 Urgent CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 4) demonstrated an intraparenchymal haematoma in the right basal ganglia, a smaller left frontal haemorrhage, and extensive hypodensity in the left supratentorial white matter suggestive of cerebral oedema.
 
 In the MRI, two weeks after the acute event and corticosteroid treatment, hyperintensities in white matter on T2-weighted (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c) and FLAIR (Figures 5d and 5e) sequences are observed, without diffusion restriction (Figures 5f and 5g), corresponding to vasogenic oedema with improvement compared to the previous CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 4).
 
 CT one month after the acute event (Figures 6a, 6b, 6c, 6d and 7) showed near-complete resolution of cerebral oedema, confirming the response to corticosteroid treatment.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 76-year-old male patient with a prior diagnosis of cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) was referred to the emergency department from a nursing home due to progressive clinical deterioration over the past 24 hours. The patient presented decreased consciousness. No fever or recent trauma was reported. An urgent CT scan was requested.
 
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT performed four years earlier at the onset of CAA showed an isolated convexity subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) in the right frontal sulcus without trauma, in a patient with mild cognitive impairment. In the MRI one month after SAH, on the susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) sequence, significant superficial haemosiderosis and multiple supra and infratentorial microhaemorrhages are observed as hypointense areas with a “blooming” effect. Urgent CT demonstrated an intraparenchymal haematoma in the right basal ganglia, a smaller left frontal haemorrhage, and extensive hypodensity in the left supratentorial white matter suggestive of cerebral oedema. In the MRI, two weeks after the acute event and corticosteroid treatment, hyperintensities in white matter on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences are observed, without diffusion restriction, corresponding to vasogenic oedema with improvement compared to the previous CT. CT one month after the acute event showed near-complete resolution of cerebral oedema, confirming the response to corticosteroid treatment.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Inflammatory cerebral amyloid angiopathy, Primary vasculitis of the central nervous system, Subacute infarction, Diffuse glioma, Autoimmune encephalitis

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 68-year-old male, a known case of chronic kidney disease (CKD) stage V on dialysis, complaints of swelling in the left forearm and difficulty in cannulation. The patient had a history of left brachiocephalic arteriovenous fistula formation with a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) brachiocephalic graft. Local examination showed dilated tortuous swellings on the left forearm reaching up to the cubital fossa.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound examination showed a relatively well-defined, predominantly hypoechoic lesion with a cystic component within, showing multiple septations and communication to the underlying vessel.
 
 Colour Doppler examination showed a typical “to-and-fro” pattern of flow (yin-yang sign) within the lesion and vessel with turbulent flow adjacent to the lesion. Communication with flow was noted between the lesion and the adjacent vessel.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: 68-year-old male, a known case of chronic kidney disease (CKD) stage V on dialysis, complaints of swelling in the left forearm and difficulty in cannulation. The patient had a history of left brachiocephalic arteriovenous fistula formation with a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) brachiocephalic graft. Local examination showed dilated tortuous swellings on the left forearm reaching up to the cubital fossa.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound examination showed a relatively well-defined, predominantly hypoechoic lesion with a cystic component within, showing multiple septations and communication to the underlying vessel. Colour Doppler examination showed a typical “to-and-fro” pattern of flow (yin-yang sign) within the lesion and vessel with turbulent flow adjacent to the lesion. Communication with flow was noted between the lesion and the adjacent vessel.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: True aneurysm, Anastomotic pseudoaneurysm (PTFE brachiocephalic bypass graft), Haematoma, Arteriovenous malformation, Infection (mycotic aneurysm), Abscess, Seroma, Lymphocele, Neoplasm

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 27-year-old pregnant woman at 12 weeks of gestation presented with a growing, painless mass in her left breast. The patient had no significant past medical history or family history of breast cancer. Clinical examination revealed a firm, mobile, non-tender mass in the retro-areolar region of the left breast.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound imaging at 12 weeks gestation showed a well-circumscribed lobulated heterogeneous mass with predominantly isoechoic echogenicity containing hyperechoic areas. No increased vascularity on colour Doppler ultrasound was noted. These features could suggest a benign mass with lactational changes (e.g., fibroadenoma, hamartoma, or galactocele) (BI-RADS category 3) (Figures 1a and 1b). At 24 weeks, the lesion grew to 100×53×114 mm (Figures 2a and 2b). At 38 weeks of gestation, the mass was occupying the whole breast. The increased vascularity on colour Doppler ultrasound is most likely due to breast engorgement during pregnancy (Figure 3). A core needle biopsy confirmed the lesion as benign with no evidence of malignancy (Figure 4).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 27-year-old pregnant woman at 12 weeks of gestation presented with a growing, painless mass in her left breast. The patient had no significant past medical history or family history of breast cancer. Clinical examination revealed a firm, mobile, non-tender mass in the retro-areolar region of the left breast.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound imaging at 12 weeks gestation showed a well-circumscribed lobulated heterogeneous mass with predominantly isoechoic echogenicity containing hyperechoic areas. No increased vascularity on colour Doppler ultrasound was noted. These features could suggest a benign mass with lactational changes (e.g., fibroadenoma, hamartoma, or galactocele) (BI-RADS category 3). At 24 weeks, the lesion grew to 100×53×114 mm. At 38 weeks of gestation, the mass was occupying the whole breast. The increased vascularity on colour Doppler ultrasound is most likely due to breast engorgement during pregnancy. A core needle biopsy confirmed the lesion as benign with no evidence of malignancy.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Fibroadenoma, Galactocele, Phyllodes tumour, Leiomyosarcoma, Adenomyoepithelioma, Myoid hamartoma of the breast (MHB), Breast carcinoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 41-year-old male presents with a history of microscopic haematuria, a palpable right flank mass, and recurrent headaches. A CT scan was performed, followed by a brain MRI to rule out tumoural pathology. The patient had no significant medical history.
 Imaging Findings: In the abdominal CT scan, multiple bilateral renal cystic lesions with septum formation were observed. The right kidney had multiple, irregular, heterogeneous lesions with significant enhancement post-contrast, and hypodense central areas related to necrosis, the biggest located in the superior pole. Similar characteristics were noted in the left kidney lesions (Figure 1). Also, multiple simple pancreatic cysts and splenic vein dilation were observed, along with pancreaticosplenic collaterals. There is a lesion in the splenic hilum that enhances and follows contrast in different phases, suggestive of a splenic hamartoma (Figure 2).
 
 The brain MRI revealed multiple heterogeneous lesions with a cystic and a solid component in the posterior fossa, showing avid and homogeneous enhancement of the solid portion, and causing vasogenic oedema and mass effect on the pons (Figures 3a, 3b, and 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old woman presented to an otorhinolaryngology consultation with a 2-month history of dysphonia and a 2-year history of gradual right hearing loss. No other symptoms were reported. Right vocal cord paralysis was noted at flexible nasofibrolaryngoscopy. A CT of the neck and chest was requested for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: CT demonstrated signs of right vocal cord paralysis, right tongue haemiatrophy, and atrophy of the right trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles (Figure 1). A lytic lesion in the right skull base was noted, only partially covered, given the undirected protocol. There were no other relevant findings.
 
 A brain and skull base CT and MRI were requested for further evaluation. CT showed a lytic lesion centred at the petroclival synchondrosis, with extensive destruction of the clivus and petrous temporal bone, showing calcified areas and involvement of the foramen jugular and carotid canal (Figure 2).
 
 At MRI, the lesion showed marked T2WI hyperintensity, T1WI hypointensity, and heterogeneous gadolinium enhancement (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). There was no restriction to the diffusion of water molecules, with high ADC values (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 The lesion infiltrated the right cavernous sinus, right Meckel’s cave, foramen rotundum, foramen ovale, and the hypoglossal canal (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old male driver presented with insidious onset pain and effusion of the right knee for 3 weeks. The patient had a similar history the previous year, which resolved after taking nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). The knee range of motion was terminally painful. There was no history of trauma, arthritis or comorbidities.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographs of the right knee revealed normal bone density without fracture or erosion. MRI of the right knee revealed patchy areas in the epimetaphyseal region of both the femoral condyles, showing hyperintense signals on proton density fat-suppressed (PDFS) images (Figure 1) and correlating hypointense signals on T1W images (Figure 2), suggestive of marrow oedema. Mild knee joint effusion was noted without synovial thickening, erosions or low signal lines. The ligaments and menisci were normal. The tibial condyles, fibula and patella showed normal marrow signal intensity. The articular cartilage, posteromedial and posterolateral corners of the knee joint were normal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old male presented to the paediatric department with a history of headache, which was severe and diffuse in nature and associated with 2–3 episodes of non-projectile vomiting. There was no associated history of blurring of vision, loss of consciousness, seizure, trauma, fever, pain abdomen or loose stool. On examination, he had an emaciated appearance with microcephaly and hyperextensible joints.
 
 The patient had developmental delay since infancy, which he gradually gained at >3 years of age. He had an episode of seizure at 6 years of age, and his EEG revealed generalised epileptiform discharges. He was started on valproate, which was stopped in 2020. He continued to have multiple episodes of headache and vomiting during this period. He presented with bilateral papilledema in September 2021.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain revealed T2 hyperintensities (Figures 1a and 1b) involving bilateral subcortical, deep, periventricular and cerebellar white matter with sparing of the perirolandic white matter. These areas show restriction of diffusion (Figures 2a and 2b) and do not show blooming on gradient echo (GRE) images. There is a prominence of bilateral optic nerves with tortuosity and optic nerve sheath dilatation (Figures 3a and 3b) with mild subluxation of both lenses (Figure 4). There is a relative increase in choline creatinine peak with preserved N-acetylaspartate (NAA) in TE 135 ms (Figure 5).
 
 Imaging done 3 months later revealed a mild reduction in global white matter hyperintensity (Figure 6).
 
 The next imaging, done 2 years later, revealed a significant reduction in the white matter hyperintensities and restriction of diffusion involving the white matter (Figure 7).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old male presented with 2 days of severe right-sided lower abdominal pain. He was pyrexial with a raised neutrophil count (9.2x10^9/L) and CRP (55mg/L). Examination revealed right inguinal swelling and tenderness. Due to the high suspicion of incarcerated inguinal hernia, a CT abdomen and pelvis was requested.
 Imaging Findings: A portal venous phase CT abdomen and pelvis found a thickened and hyper-enhancing right spermatic cord, with the supra-scrotal vas deferens measuring up to 5mm. Further inflammation was seen within the inguinal canal, and a small reactive hydrocele was noted. A unilaterally enlarged right seminal vesicle was identified, however, there was no abscess.
 
 On the assumption that these findings were due to infection, oral ciprofloxacin was commenced, with temperatures and pain settling within the first 24 hours. The patient has not had any subsequent presentations. A right inguinal hernia repair was also performed during the inpatient stay with antibiotic cover to reduce the risk of infection. There were no complications.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 51-year-old male patient presented with right-sided limb weakness and slurred speech for a month. No vomiting/seizures/syncope was reported.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed a well-defined lesion in the left periventricular region, measuring 1.8 x 1.5 x 1.6 cm (AP x ML x CC) with significant perifocal oedema (Figure 1). The lesion was T2 hetero-intense (Figure 1) with a T2 hypointense and T1 hyperintense rim (Figure 2), showing diffusion restriction (Figure 4). Associated mass effects noted were midline shift to the right, left uncal herniation, and effacement of the left lateral ventricle (Figure 6). Extensive vasogenic oedema was noted involving the left gangliocapsular region, left frontotemporal lobe, left hemi-midbrain, and left hemi-pons (Figure 1). On post-contrast images, the lesion showed heterogenous enhancement with involvement of the left lateral ventricular margin (Figure 3).
 
 Tractography showed interruption of white matter tracts in the region of the lesion (Figure 5a). The region of perilesional oedema showed only deviation of the left internal capsule and left superior longitudinal fasciculus tracts (Figure 5a), explaining the patient’s symptoms. Fractional anisotropy was decreased (Figure 5b) while mean diffusivity was increased (Figure 5c) within the lesion and in the region of perilesional oedema.
 
 The patient then underwent left FP craniotomy and excision of the lesion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman came to the emergency department with complaints of multiple bone pains. She reported fractures of the right forearm and the right leg three years ago, occurring without any significant trauma. Laboratory findings revealed elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP > 1400) and parathyroid hormone (720 pg/ml), serum calcium level was 10.8 mg/dl, and serum creatinine level was 0.9. There was no skin lesion.
 Imaging Findings: Skeletal Survey (Radiograph of the skull, dorso-lumbar spine and both upper and lower extremities)
 
 The shafts of the long bones showed ground glass opacities with expansion of the medullary cavity and thinning of the cortex. There were multiple lytic lesions in the shaft of the long bones, and a few of them showed a sclerotic rim. There were areas of sclerosis in the metaphysis of the long bones (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). The dorsal lumbar spine showed reduced bone density with endplate sclerosis, consistent with a rugger-jersey spine (Figure 2b). Multiple lytic lesions were seen in the scapula, ribs, clavicle, multiple vertebral bodies, pelvic bones and metacarpals on both sides (Figures 2a and 2c). There was subperiosteal resorption on the radial aspect of the head of the second proximal phalanx (Figure 3). Skull radiograph showed a widening of the diploic spaces with multiple well-defined, variable-sized sclerotic lesions (Figure 4).
 
 CT Brain
 
 The CT brain revealed a widening of diploic spaces with multiple well-defined sclerotic and ground glass density lesions (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c).
 
 USG Neck
 
 In the US of the neck, there was a well-defined heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion posterior to the right lobe of the thyroid (Figure 6).
 
 CT Parathyroid Protocol
 
 A hypodense enhancing lesion posterior to the right lobe of the thyroid was detected, suggestive of parathyroid adenoma (Figures 7a, 7b, 8a, 8b, 9a, 9b, 9c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old woman presented with permanent pain of increasing intensity in her right leg that had been evolving for 24 hours. The pain appeared immediately following a horse ride in an improperly adjusted stirrup and was not relieved by paracetamol, ketoprofen and morphine. No significant medical history.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the legs showed a swollen appearance of the right anterolateral compartment muscles with:
 
 Fat-suppressed coronal T2-weighted (Figure 1) and axial PD-SPIR (Figure 2) emphasised the acute involvement of the lateral compartment of the leg through a noticeable bulging of the compartment muscles, which is clearly visible in the axial view when compared to the healthy contralateral side. Additional loss of fascicular architecture is seen. Diffuse hyperintensity of the compartment indicates muscular oedema.
 
 Sagittal T1-weighted sequence showed slight spontaneous high signal intensity within the involved muscles (Figure 3).
 
 After injection, the fat-suppressed axial T1-weighted sequence (Figure 4) showed focal defects of enhancement within the proximal portion of the muscles, indicating areas of ischemia.
 
 No other causes of acute pain, including a stress fracture, deep vein thrombosis or focal muscular strain, were identified on the examination. The diagnosis of acute compartment syndrome was confirmed by MRI.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 57-year-old woman presents with a left preauricular lump of two weeks’ evolution. Physical examination reveals a soft, mobile 1 cm left preauricular lump upon superficial palpation. An MRI and a fine-needle aspiration biopsy was requested for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Facial MRI revealed a small lesion in the superficial lobe of the left parotid gland, located cranial to the Stensen duct, and anterior and lateral to the facial nerve, showing mild heterogeneity and peripheral enhancement after contrast injection. It exhibited a very low apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) value on the ADC map (0.4 to 0.5x10^-3 mm
 
 2
 
 /second) and a type III TIC (time-intensity curve), suggesting radiological aggressiveness (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, 1d, and 1e).
 
 Additionally, a marked enlargement of the left lacrimal gland was observed, with similar radiological findings on diffusion and dynamic gadolinium-enhancement sequences, indicative of an infiltrative process (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, and 2e).
 
 The low ADC values on both lesions gave the clue to diagnose a possible concomitant lymphoproliferative process, and this finding is key to the diagnosis [1].

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old female patient presented with complaints of fever, altered sensorium and convulsions with a short history of 2 days. Her vitals were normal. She was further advised for an MRI brain non-contrast study for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: In this patient, the MRI brain non-contrast study was performed, and it revealed T2 hyperintense signal abnormality with FLAIR hyperintense signal changes involving bilateral medial temporal lobes. No abnormal post-contrast neuro-parenchymal or leptomeningeal enhancement was noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 60-year-old female presented to the casualty with an episode of loss of consciousness for a period of thirty minutes. After regaining consciousness, the patient lost power in all four limbs and was unable to walk without assistance.
 Imaging Findings: On NCCT (non-contrast computed tomography) brain, multiple small hypodense cystic lesions were noted, many showing scolex inside depicted by the hyperdense eccentric dot within and surrounding oedema (Figures 1a and 1b). A few of them were seen with calcifications inside, suggesting calcified scolices, thus describing the different stages of the disease. A sub-centimetric calcified lesion was seen in the intermuscular plane between the left semispinalis capitis and trapezius muscle (Figures 1a and 1b). On MRI correlation, multiple well-defined sub-centimetric hyperintense lesions were noted in bilateral cerebral hemispheres. Internal eccentric scolex was noted; however, no evidence of mass effect was seen (Figures 2a and 2b). On X-ray examination, multiple calcified lesions were seen in the intermuscular plane along bilateral proximal lower limbs, suggesting disseminated disease and rice grain calcifications (Figure 3). Further ultrasonographic evaluation depicted a non-compressible, hypoechoic cystic lesion with a central calcification in the gracilis muscle along the medial compartment of the thigh (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old female with a history of hypertension, lower back pain from multiple older compression fractures and long-term hyponatraemia was referred for a thoracic and abdominal CT scan with contrast to rule out malignancy. The present case reports an incidental finding with no correlation to the abovementioned symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Initial contrast CT showed a local, rounded, well-defined mass measuring 30 mm x 52 mm x 41 mm (AP x CC x TR), with a low-density fat component, a soft tissue component and no cortical erosion, destruction or scalloping (Figures 1, 2, and 3). In close proximity to the mass, a marginally enlarged lymph node was located cranially to the left of the mass (Figure 3).
 
 MRI with T1-weighted (T1W) (Figure 4) and STIR (Figure 5) images showed the presacral mass to have a high heterogeneous signal, but with a fat signal, and the mass to be well defined and with high probability of being encapsulated. Additionally, T1-fat saturation weighted sequence without and with contrast (Figures 6, 7, and 8) showed the mass to be of high fat content, showing discrete linear strands of contrast enhancement in the mass, but no nodular enhancement. MRI also confirmed no presence of bone marrow oedema.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A female patient in her 50s with a known diagnosis of melanoma of the pectoral region underwent a follow-up PET/CT.
 Imaging Findings: The PET/CT revealed suspicious lung nodules and left axillary lymphadenopathy, and an area of abnormal uptake in the gallbladder (Figure 1). Subsequent CT showed an endoluminal polypoid lesion with heterogeneous arterial enhancement. There was no evidence of invasion into the adjacent hepatic parenchyma or extension to the surrounding fat (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male with moderate to severe sensorineural hearing loss.
 Imaging Findings: The cochlea demonstrates an absent modiolus, while the interscalar septa remain intact, creating a characteristic “corkscrew” appearance. The cochlear aperture is enlarged, the modiolus is absent, and a widened internal auditory canal is absent (Figures 1 and 2).
PostDescription:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old female patient presented with exertional dyspnoea, weakness, abdominal pain, and lower limb swelling persisting for a month. There was no history of fever. Initial ultrasound investigation revealed mild ascites, multiple small abdominal lymphadenopathies and minimal pericardial effusion. Laboratory results showed haemoglobin of 12.2g/dL, platelet count of 105×109/L and white blood cell count of 3.32×10
 
 9
 
 /L. A 12-lead ECG revealed prolonged P waves, abnormal Q waves, poor R wave progression, and T wave abnormalities (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography revealed mild pericardial effusion, grade II diastolic dysfunction, right ventricle with segmental aneurysmal dilation and apical retraction, mild mitral regurgitation, and hyperechoic mass at the left ventricular apex, indicative of fibrofatty tissue deposition. While the right ventricle (RV) showed localised enlargement due to aneurysm formation, the overall restrictive physiology remained evident (Figures 2a and 2b). No thrombus was detected on echocardiography and cardiac MRI. Chest X-ray showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette and elevated diaphragms.
 
 The patient was referred for a cardiac MRI in our department. Cardiac MRI, done with cine imaging, perfusion, and early and late gadolinium-enhanced (LGE) PSIR sequences on MAGNETOM Vida 3T scanner revealed ascites, pericardial effusion, enlarged right atrium, retraction of the RV apex (RV apical notch), RV aneurysm, obliteration of the left ventricular (LV) apex on True FISP (true fast imaging with steady-state free precession) cine imaging (Figure 3). The LV end-diastolic volume was 69 mL, and the end-systolic volume was 28 mL. Similarly, the RV end-diastolic volume measured 70 mL, with an end-systolic volume of 39 mL, indicating relatively small chamber volumes. LGE imaging demonstrated subendocardial fibrosis with fat infiltration at both the LV and RV apices, resulting in a V-shaped enhancement pattern in each ventricle (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 12-year-old Malagasy boy consulted for a fever that had been evolving for two months, with a notion of trauma and pain in the right upper quadrant as well as chronic diarrhoea. A parasitological examination of the stool found eggs of
 
 Schistosoma mansoni
 
 . An ultrasound and a CT scan were then ordered.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (Figure 1), there were multiple confluent hypoechoic nodular formations, some of which have peripheral hyperechoic walls in segment VII of the liver. On CT scan (Figures 2 and 3), the formation corresponds to multiple hypodense fluid collections with thick peripheral enhancing walls with peri-lesional oedema in segment VII of the liver. There was an extracapsular extension into the hepato-renal space. The liver was enlarged, measuring 187 mm in the midclavicular line. The spleen was also increased in size, measuring 160 mm in height. The rest of the examination was considered normal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old boy was brought by his parents with complaints of pain and swelling in the right hip and right iliac fossa since three months. He had difficulty in walking since one month. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness at the right hip.
 Imaging Findings: A pelvic radiograph revealed a soft tissue opacity in the right iliac fossa, erosion of the right sacroiliac (SI) joint and the right sacral ala, with the right hip joint abducted and externally rotated (Figure 1).
 
 CT demonstrated a heterogeneously hypodense lesion originating from the right sacrum, with a large exophytic pelvic component and soft tissue extension into the right SI joint (Figures 2a and 2b), displacing bowel loops and the bladder to the left (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 MRI revealed a T1/T2 heterogeneously hyperintense soft tissue lesion from the right sacrum with an exophytic pelvic component and multiple cystic areas suggesting blood degradation products (Figures 4a and 4b). There were diffusion-restricted areas (Figures 5a and 5b), extension into the S1 and S2 neural foramina with nerve root encasement (Figure 6), and heterogeneous post-contrast enhancement (Figure 7).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old male presented to the medical OPD with complaints of ataxia, multiple episodes of falls while walking, and bed wetting for a one-year duration, with gradually progressive symptoms. On examination, the patient had shuffling gait, tremors, and cogwheel rigidity. Nerve conduction studies, parathyroid hormone assay and calcium studies were within normal limits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT head revealed bilaterally symmetrical hyperdense areas in bilateral basal ganglia, thalami, subcortical white matter, red nucleus, dentate nucleus and bilateral cerebellar hemispheres (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 MRI brain revealed bilaterally symmetrical areas that were hyperintense on T1WI, hypointense on T2WI, and showing blooming on gradient echo sequences (GRE) images in the bilateral basal ganglia, thalami (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c), bilateral red nuclei (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), bilateral dentate nuclei and cerebellar hemispheres (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c), and subcortical white matter (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c). These areas did not show restriction of diffusion on DWI images. There were discrete and confluent areas of T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in periventricular and deep white matter of bilateral frontoparietal lobes, showing no restriction of diffusion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old male patient with no significant past medical background is referred for left knee pain since the age of 10, associated with knee swelling. Since then, he has always maintained complaints but lived a normal life. Plain radiograph, CT scan and MR were ordered for further investigation.
 Imaging Findings: On plain film (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d), there are periosteal and endosteal cortical thickening associated with extra-cortical bone formation along the anterior aspect of the left tibia and the medial aspect of the left femur. Areas of central medullary hyperostosis can be observed in the inferior third of the femoral diaphysis and in the femoral head. The lesions have an appearance that resembles “flowing candle wax”.
 
 CT scan (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, and 2e) allows for further acknowledgement of the changes described above.
 
 MR performed on a 1.5T MR scanner (T1 and STIR sequences in axial and coronal planes; Figures 3a and 3b) depict extensive changes with very low signal on all imaging sequences, mainly involving the left femur and tibia, with diffuse thickening of the bone cortex giving the bone surface a wavy appearance, as well as multiple intramedullary foci showing marked hypointensity, indicating a strong component of bone sclerosis. The lesions show eccentric growth, extending from the diaphysis to the epiphysis, and exceeding the cortical surface.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 30‐year‐old woman (P1L1) presented with a two‐day history of acute pelvic pain and vomiting. There was no history of fever or vaginal bleeding. Her menstrual cycles were regular, but often accompanied by mild dysmenorrhea. No history of tubal ligation. The patient’s medical history was unremarkable, and a urinary pregnancy test was negative.
 Imaging Findings: Transvaginal sonography (TVS) revealed a convoluted, heterogeneously echogenic mass in the pouch of Douglas (POD), predominantly towards the right, showing significant surrounding inflammation. At its distal end was a well-defined, hypoechoic cystic lesion measuring approximately 3×4 cm, exhibiting peripheral mucosal infoldings with a classic cog-wheel pattern, strongly indicating tubal origin. The cyst contained homogeneous low-level internal echoes, raising suspicion for a haematosalpinx.
 
 Doppler imaging demonstrated a twisted vascular pedicle with a near-classic whirlpool pattern, along with pronounced adnexal hyperaemia. The right ovary maintained its normal anatomical position, morphology and good stromal vascularity, however, with mild periovarian hyperaemia. A small volume of echogenic free fluid was present in the POD.
 
 The left ovary, adnexa, uterus and cervix appeared unremarkable, while the endometrium exhibited features of hyperplasia.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old non-obese female with a history of cholecystectomy in the last four months presents to the emergency department with clinical signs including right upper quadrant pain, generalised jaundice, and a fever of 38.5°C. Liver function tests reveal hepatic cytolysis and cholestasis (elevated AST/ALT and ALP). An abdominal ultrasound was requested.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal ultrasound with a low-frequency probe reveals the presence of multiple hyperechoic formations within the intrahepatic bile ducts, causing posterior acoustic shadowing (Figure 1) and resulting in dilatation of the intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 2 and 3). The common bile duct was clear.
 
 The presence of jaundice and fever raised concerns about an associated cholangitis, prompting the indication for hepatobiliary MRI. The MRI reveals more pronounced dilatation of the intrahepatic bile ducts, particularly in the left lobe of the liver, containing multiple signal-void formations consistent with the previously described biliary calculi (Figures 4 and 5). Additionally, T1-weighted sequences post-gadolinium demonstrate subtle thickening and enhancement of the intrahepatic bile duct walls along with peribiliary hepatic infiltration, secondary to the inflammation of the liver adjacent to biliary ducts caused by potential cholangitis (Figure 6).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously healthy 53-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with an episode of dysarthria followed by a decrease in the level of consciousness, without sphincter involvement. Physical examination upon admission was unremarkable, with no signs of neurological focal deficits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced CT shows two hypodense frontal lesions involving the cortex and the subcortical white matter with minimal surrounding oedema (Figure 1). The right lesion presents with a gyriform coarse calcification, and there is no enhancement after intravenous contrast administration (Figure 2). The left lesion produces light bone remodelling.
 
 On MRI, the lesions exhibit heterogeneous signal intensity and ill-defined margins, predominantly showing hyperintensity on T2/FLAIR sequences without communication between them through the corpus callosum (Figures 3a and 3b), and areas of hypointensity on susceptibility-weighted imaging consistent with coarse calcifications on the right side and bleeding on the left side (Figures 4a and 4b). There is no significant diffusion restriction (Figures 5a and 5b). Following contrast administration, only minimal linear enhancement is observed on both lesions (Figure 6).
 
 A perfusion study was carried out showing an increase in the cerebral blood volume (Figures 7a and 7b), and spectroscopy reveals a decrease in the N-acetylaspartate (N-Ace) peak, no elevation of lactate (LL) and an increased choline (Ch) level (Figures 8a and 8b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old woman presented with epigastric pain and uneasiness since six months. She had no difficulty in swallowing or regurgitation of food. She had no respiratory complaints. An ultrasonography of the abdomen was unremarkable. The patient was subjected to an erect chest radiograph for routine evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Erect frontal chest radiograph showed a well-defined, lobulated, homogeneous, soft tissue opacity (green arrow) in the left lower hemithorax, obscuring the medial aspect of the left dome of the diaphragm. It was seen as continuous with an ill-defined, left paravertebral soft tissue opacity (pink arrow). There was no silhouetting of the left heart border (Figure 1).
 
 A barium (95% w/v) oesophagogram showed unobstructed passage of barium through the thoracic soft tissue opacity. The retrocardiac oesophagus was tortuous and deviated to the left with a normal gastro-oesophageal junction (Figure 2).
 
 An unenhanced CT of the chest was performed with oral contrast, followed by intravenous contrast-enhanced CT with oral contrast in the same sitting. Axial unenhanced images showed circumferential, homogeneous thickening of the lower oesophageal wall with patency of the lumen showing orally administered water-soluble contrast. There were a few tiny foci of calcification within the wall (pink arrow on Figure 3). Sagittal venous phase images showed homogeneous enhancement of the thickened oesophageal wall without areas of necrosis (blue arrow) or evidence of invasion into surrounding structures (Figure 4). Coronal unenhanced images in the lung window showed surrounding atelectasis without signs of lung parenchymal involvement (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old male patient complains of long-lasting chest pain, which worsens in the prone position and interferes with sleep. He denies any dyspnoea, palpitations, or a history of trauma. His medical history includes anxiety and a surgical procedure to remove multiple bone tumours from his lower limbs six years ago.
 Imaging Findings: A computed tomography (CT) scan was performed on the chest, revealing two lesions on the left scapula: one on the ventral surface in the subscapular fossa and another on the dorsal surface in the infraspinatus fossa (Figures 1 and 2). Both lesions presented calcium density and were found to have continuity with the adjacent bone, assuming a pedunculated conformation with anterior and posterior protrusion. These well-defined lesions did not show destruction of the cortical bone, irregular calcifications, or periosteal reaction of the underlying bone.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 66-year-old man with no significant medical history develops back pain after a running session. After starting physiotherapy, the complaints became acutely worse and did not disappear during the night. No other B-symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: Computer tomography (CT) of the thoracic spine showed a moth-eaten osteolytic aspect of the 7th thoracic vertebral body with a pathological wedge-shaped compression fracture. There is an associated homogeneously enhancing extensive paravertebral and epidural soft tissue component (approximately 56 x 65 x 47 mm) with encasement of the azygos vein.
 
 MRI demonstrated diffuse T1 hypointense, STIR hyperintense (compared to the disci) bone marrow infiltration of the 7th vertebral body with paravertebral and epidural mass extension. The mass showed homogeneous contrast enhancement with marked diffusion restriction (ADC value of 710 μm²/sec). A compressive myelopathy developed based on the epidural component. Encasement of the azygos vein is seen, as well as intercostal arteries traversing through the mass without invasion. There were no signs of invasion of the disci or other vertebrae.
 
 Additional CT thorax and abdomen, PET-CT, and whole body MRI showed no other lesions or adenopathies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old lady presented with complaints of pain and swelling in the left-hand middle finger following minor trauma. She has been having on-and-off dull aching pain. She sought treatment in a local hospital with buddy strapping. The blood investigation showed mildly elevated CRP (5.8 mg/dl) and ESR (50 mm/hr) but normal serum creatinine, calcium, phosphorus and alkaline phosphatase.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray showed a well-defined central expansile lytic lesion with scalloped margins involving the base of the 3rd proximal phalanx of the left hand with few specks of calcifications, and pathological fractures involving medial and lateral walls of the 3rd proximal phalanx (Figure 1a). Computed tomography showed similar findings to X-ray (Figure 1b). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the hand showed a well-defined expansile lytic lesion which was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, and showed no diffusion restriction with scalloping and fracture involving medial and lateral walls, causing splaying of sagittal bands on both sides (Figures 1c, 1d, 1e, 1f, and 1g).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old female patient, with no relevant previous history, presented to the outpatient clinic complaining of a progressively growing swelling at the left popliteal fossa, with associated limited knee flexion and local pain. At clinical examination, a firm and non-mobile swelling was evident at the popliteal region on the left.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed a subfascial soft tissue mass mainly composed of tissue isointense to the muscle on T1-weighted (T1) sequences, hyperintense on T2-weighted fat-suppressed (T2FS) sequences and which, after contrast with fat suppression (T1 Gd), showed intense and fast enhancement, as well as “feathery” margins (flame sign). These areas were mixed with areas of very low signal on T1 and T2FS, which did not enhance after contrast (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 2). Some diffusion restriction was seen in the more enhancing areas (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 The extra-articular mass extended into the subcutis, as well as into the muscle bellies of the biceps femoris and medial gastrocnemius. The mass also encased the peroneal nerve (Figure 4), following its trajectory until the level of the fibular head (Figures 5a and 5b). Reactive oedema along the deep fascia of the lower leg was also present.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old female patient, with no relevant previous history, presented to the outpatient clinic complaining of a progressively growing swelling at the left popliteal fossa, with associated limited knee flexion and local pain. At clinical examination, a firm and non-mobile swelling was evident at the popliteal region on the left.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed a subfascial soft tissue mass mainly composed of tissue isointense to the muscle on T1-weighted (T1) sequences, hyperintense on T2-weighted fat-suppressed (T2FS) sequences and which, after contrast with fat suppression (T1 Gd), showed intense and fast enhancement, as well as “feathery” margins (flame sign). These areas were mixed with areas of very low signal on T1 and T2FS, which did not enhance after contrast. Some diffusion restriction was seen in the more enhancing areas. The extra-articular mass extended into the subcutis, as well as into the muscle bellies of the biceps femoris and medial gastrocnemius. The mass also encased the peroneal nerve, following its trajectory until the level of the fibular head. Reactive oedema along the deep fascia of the lower leg was also present.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Desmoid fibromatosis, Fibrosarcoma, Soft tissue sarcoma, Solitary fibrous tumour

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old female presented with a 3-day history of upper abdominal pain and nausea without vomiting, bowel changes, or systemic symptoms. Examination revealed epigastric tenderness without peritoneal signs. Laboratory results showed mild elevated CRP (16 mg/L) and WCC (11.9 x 10⁹/L), with normal liver functions and amylase levels.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed ill-defined fat stranding in the lesser omentum, located near the lesser curvature of the stomach. This fat stranding was not associated with a well-defined mass. The stomach and pancreas appeared normal on imaging, except for a small hiatus hernia identified incidentally. There was no evidence of free intraperitoneal gas, bowel wall thickening, or signs of bowel obstruction. No other abnormalities were observed in the abdominal and pelvic structures, and no additional masses or lesions were noted.
