OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 30‐year‐old man presented with two months of right knee pain and swelling. He had recovered from COVID‑19 pneumonia three months earlier after receiving systemic steroids. During his stay in the hospital, the patient has received dexamethasone injection 8 mg/day through the intravenous route for 10 days with gradual tapering over the next 2 weeks. No evidence of diabetes mellitus or other systemic comorbidities. Localised tenderness over the patella was noted; there were no systemic symptoms or history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographs of the knee in anteroposterior, lateral, and skyline views revealed superficial sclerosis of the patella, along with a subtle erosion at its inferior pole (Figures 1a and 1b). There were no signs of fracture or significant soft tissue injury. Given the clinical context and radiographic findings, infection was suspected, and an MRI was recommended.
 
 On MRI, the patellar inferior pole appeared hypointense on T1 images (Figures 2a and 2b) and hyperintense on proton density (PD) fat-saturated sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Diffusion-weighted images (DWI) also indicated restriction (Figures 4a and 4b). The subchondral bone and articular cartilage remained largely intact, although a mild effusion was present. Post-contrast images showed thickening and enhancement of the synovium (Figures 5a and 5b). Osteomyelitis of the patella was considered likely.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old female presented with a longstanding history of primary amenorrhea and cyclical lower abdominal pain since adolescence. The pain occurs every month, typically lasting 3–5 days, and is described as crampy and localised to the lower abdomen and pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound examination done on a Samsung RS80A ultrasound machine showed a bulky uterine body with hypoechoic endometrial collection (Figure 1). A heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with internal echogenic debris was noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figure 2). The left ovary appears normal.
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed using a 1.5 Tesla GE MR machine. T2-weighted sagittal and axial images of the MRI pelvis show the presence of the fundus and body of the uterus, which is oriented in the right lateral position in the pelvic cavity, with the absence of the cervix (Figure 3). However, the vagina is visualised. T1 hyperintense and T2 mildly hypointense endometrial collection is noted within the endometrial cavity (Figures 3 and 4). A well-defined T1 hyperintense and predominantly T2 hypointense (T2 shading sign) cystic lesion with a thin wall is noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figures 5 and 6). Minimal pelvic ascites is noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old man known to have hypertension and type 2 diabetes experienced paraesthesia in his left hand and distal part of his left forearm for several months. On examination, multiple well-defined skin nodules were found, globally distributed, along with reduced sensation distally in the left upper limb.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus with intravenous contrast showed multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular part of the left brachial plexus, including the trunks and divisions (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 3), extending into the axilla and proximal parts of the upper arm, suggestive of neurofibromas.
 
 MRI of the spine and brain showed no abnormalities, including vestibular schwannoma, and the patient was diagnosed with schwannomatosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 60-year-old female with a known history of chronic kidney disease, on monthly haemodialysis and type 2 diabetes mellitus, presented with confusion and lethargy for the past 2 days. There was no history of trauma, fever, or seizures. On examination, the patient was disoriented but exhibited no focal neurological deficits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT (Figure 1) shows bilateral symmetric hypodensity in the lentiform nuclei, with surrounding more hypodense internal and external capsules forming the “lentiform fork sign”. There is a sub-centimetric-sized subcortical calcification in the right parietal lobe.
 
 MRI brain T2-weighted (Figure 2) shows bilateral symmetric hyperintensity in the thalamus, lentiform nuclei, particularly in the putamen and globus pallidus, with marked symmetrical hyperintensity of the internal, external capsules and internal and external medullary laminae.
 
 MRI FLAIR axial section (Figure 3) through the basal ganglia shows symmetric hyperintensity in the external capsule, internal capsule and internal and external medullary laminae on both sides. There is also hyperintensity in bilateral thalamus, putamen and globus pallidi.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 59-year-old man who had undergone a bilateral vasectomy 20 years earlier underwent a scrotal ultrasound due to mild right testicular discomfort. There was no history of trauma or associated symptoms, and no relevant physical examination or laboratory findings. He had no history of travel outside Europe.
 Imaging Findings: An ultrasound scan using a high-frequency linear transducer (Figures 1a, 1b, and 2) revealed multiple tiny hyperechoic foci within the left epididymal body, measuring less than 1 mm and exhibiting continuous oscillatory movement. The right epididymis had a homogeneous echotexture and normal, symmetric vascularisation using Doppler ultrasound. No abnormal findings were observed in the remaining scrotal ultrasound, including the right testicle and epididymis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 22-year-old female presented with dysphagia for 9 years and gradually increasing vulval swelling for 5 years. Clinical examination revealed a firm, non-tender, lobulated mass (~8x8 cm) involving bilateral labia with intact overlying skin. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy showed a bulge in the mid and lower oesophagus with intact mucosa and luminal narrowing.
 Imaging Findings: MRI thorax showed a long segment bulky circumferential wall thickening of the lower thoracic oesophagus extending till the gastric cardia (Figure 1c), with T1 isointense (Figure 1a) and T2 mildly hyperintense appearance (Figure 1b) compared to the skeletal muscle. It shows mild homogeneous enhancement without necrotic areas (Figure 1d), a very smooth outline and a clear plane with the aorta, cardia and thoracic spine.
 
 MRI pelvis showed a large, lobulated, well-circumscribed mass (~9x7 cm) involving the clitoral region and bilateral labia majora. It appears T1 isointense (Figure 2b) and T2 hyperintense compared to that of skeletal muscle (Figures 2a and 2d), it shows diffusion restriction (Figures 2e and 2f) and a marked homogeneous contrast enhancement (Figure 2c). The mass was seen overhanging the urethral orifice and vaginal introitus without causing urinary outflow obstruction.
 
 Non-contrast PET-CT showed
 
 FDG-avid soft tissue lesions corresponding to the oesophageal wall thickening (SUV max = 9.2) (Figures 1e and 1f) and vulval mass (SUV max = 9.8) (Figures 2g and 2h). There were no calcifications, intralesional fat, or cystic areas within the lesions.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-month-old infant presented with a history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek since 2 months (Figure 1). No history of trauma. Full-term normal delivery.
 Imaging Findings: Head and neck CT with bone reconstruction showed cortical irregularity with sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increase in cortical width and density, seen along the angle and ramus of the left hemimandible (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 On MRI, the corresponding area showed mild STIR hyperintensity (Figure 3) and T1 hypointensity (Figure 4). No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region (Figures 5a and 5b). Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-month-old infant presented with a history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek since 2 months. No history of trauma. Full-term normal delivery.
 Imaging Findings: Head and neck CT with bone reconstruction showed cortical irregularity with sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increase in cortical width and density, seen along the angle and ramus of the left hemimandible. On MRI, the corresponding area showed mild STIR hyperintensity and T1 hypointensity. No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region. Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Osteomyelitis, Battered baby syndrome, Hypervitaminosis A, Hyperphosphataemia, Ewing sarcoma, Scurvy, Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease), Malignant infantile hyperpetrosis, Prolonged PGE1 infusion

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement (Figure 1). A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other (Figures 2a and 2b). The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals (Figure 2c). The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance (Figure 3).
 
 Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers (Figure 4a). A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly (Figure 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old male presented to the emergency department with weakness and paraesthesia in the lower limbs, back pain, and fever. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed an increased white blood cell count (predominantly polymorphonuclear) and protein. He had a history of gonococcal urethritis and syphilis and a recent influenza B respiratory infection.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) shows a longitudinally extensive posterior extradural extramedullary collection from T2 to T9 of heterogeneous appearance with isointense and hyperintense areas in T1-weighted sequence (Figure 1) and high signal intensity on T2-weighted and STIR sequences (Figure 2). It causes a compressive effect on the spinal cord with anterior thecal sac displacement (Figure 3). There is increased STIR and T2 cord signal in the compressed segment, suggestive of compressive myelopathy (Figure 2). The contrast-enhanced T1-weighted sequence shows diffuse meningeal spinal enhancement (Figures 4, 5a and 5b). The magnetic field strength of the MRI scanner used was 3T.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old female neonate born through normal vaginal delivery to non-consanguineous marriage was found to have dark pigmentation in the groin, perineum, lower back, upper and lower extremities, including hands, with hair growth in these areas (Figure 1). No other abnormalities on clinical examination and blood workup. This finding raised suspicion of phacomatosis, prompting a brain MRI to assess for neuroparenchymal abnormalities.
 Imaging Findings: Multifocal punctate T1 hyperintense foci scattered in bilateral cerebral cortices, left amygdala and left cerebellar hemisphere (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). These did not show restricted diffusion or blooming on SWI. No signal changes were seen on T2 (Figure 3). No leptomeningeal T1 hyperintensities were seen. Post-contrast images showed no leptomeningeal or neuroparenchymal enhancement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old boy presented with a chronic history of rhinosinusitis and productive cough for years. There were no associated symptoms of haemoptysis, fever, or weight loss. On examination, he had mild basal crepitations. Otherwise, no dysmorphic features or dextrocardia. No palpable organomegaly or lymphadenopathy. History of paternal aunt infertility was reported.
 Imaging Findings: Initial chest radiograph showed mild right lower zone faint opacification with no dextrocardia (Figure 1). The high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) revealed mild bilateral lower lobe predominant bronchiectasis with bronchial wall thickening and mucoid impaction as well as centrilobular nodules (Figures 2a and 2b). The rest of the lung parenchyma was well aerated. No situs inversus or dextrocardia was seen. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
 
 Paranasal sinuses (PNS) CT revealed near complete opacification of the paranasal sinuses with frothy secretions as well as associated pharyngitis and left-sided otomastoiditis (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously well 12-year-old male presented to the accident and emergency department of a regional paediatric hospital with a 2-week history of worsening bilateral lower limb paraesthesia in the form of loss of fine touch, vibration and proprioception from the level of T5 distally, signifying mass effect on the dorsal columns with sparing of pain and temperature sensation. He also had reduced motor function of the lower limbs with clonus and brisk reflexes.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain was normal. MRI of the whole spine (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, 1d, and 1e) demonstrated a large solitary solid cystic lesion, centred over the spinous process and the lamina of T5 bilaterally, more extensive on the right. The solid lesion is iso/mildly hypointense to bone, with areas of T1 and T2 hypointense signal, suggestive of calcifications. The solid component measured 3.4 x 3.7 x 4.4 cm (transverse x anteroposterior x craniocaudal) and demonstrated avid enhancement. The mass is expansile with cortical expansion and trabecular replacement. It extends into the spinal canal, causing severe canal stenosis and cord compression. There was evidence of bone marrow oedema within the body of T5. No other intra or extramedullary lesions were identified.
 
 A follow-up CT thorax, abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a and 2b) with bony reformats demonstrated an expansile lytic lesion with cortical expansion involving the posterior elements of T5. There was no bony destruction. There are areas of calcification seen within the internal osseous matrix. No other bony lesions were identified, and no abnormality of note was found in the thorax, abdomen or pelvis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 15-year-old female presented with a two-year history of progressively enlarging, painful swellings on both sides of the neck, along with a recent onset of abdominal pain of one week’s duration. Examination revealed multiple palpable swellings in the axillary, cervical and inguinal regions as well as café-au-lait spots over the extremities and trunk.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography of the neck revealed multiple oval and spindle-shaped hypoechoic lesions in the bilateral posterior triangle (Figures 1a and 1b) and the anterior neck region. Similar subcutaneous lesions were noted in bilateral level 4 regions (Figures 1c and 1d), abutting carotid vessels.
 
 The contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) abdomen revealed heterogeneously enhancing oval-shaped lesions abutting the duodenojejunal flexure (Figures 2a and 2b) and a similar enhancing oblong lesion in the pelvis, posterior to the rectum at L5–S1 level (Figures 3a,3b, and 3c). Multiple hypodense, hypoenhancing nodules were imaged in the mesentery encasing the inferior mesenteric artery from its origin (Figures 4a and 4b), and extending into the pelvis and bilateral retroperitoneum. Similar nodular lesions were seen extending from the neural foramina of the lumbar spine, extending along the course of nerve roots into sciatica foramina and along the sciatic nerve (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c). A few small nodules were also noted adjacent to bilateral femoral vessels (Figures 6a and 6b). Multiple variable-sized hypodense subcutaneous nodules were noted diffusely scattered in the abdominal wall.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 59-year-old male agricultural worker from a rural area, with a long history of barefoot fieldwork, presented with a long-standing history of swelling over the plantar surface of the left foot, now progressing to involve the dorsal surface and with a history of a discharging sinus. The patient denies any history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: A clinical image of the plantar (Figure 1a) and dorsal surface (Figure 1b) of the left foot shows multiple soft and firm nodules that were nontender, along with no discharging sinus at present.
 
 X-ray of the left foot (Figure 2) and lateral views show soft-tissue density over the lateral aspect of the left foot with cortical remodelling of the diaphysis of the 4th metatarsal.
 
 Ultrasound shows multiple cystic lesions, with a few of them showing tiny echogenic grains in the dependent portion (dot in a circle) (arrow in Figure 3).
 
 On T2W sagittal (Figure 4a), soft tissue mass shows conglomerates of small (2–5 mm), rounded, hyperintense lesions with few of them showing central low-signal intensity dots and peripheral low-signal intensity rim around these hyperintense areas, representing a “dot-in-circle” sign. A few cystic areas are noted within this mass, which contains T2 hypointense foci—likely spores. On post-contrast T1 fat-saturated (FS) coronal image (Figure 4b), the involved region of soft tissue shows rim enhancement around the granulation tissue along with minimal enhancement of the central dot. There is sparing of the underlying bones and tendons. No evidence of marrow oedema noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A man in his mid-20s with a 2–3 week history of intermittent severe headache, photophobia, nausea and vomiting, presented with generalised seizures. Treatment for meningoencephalitis was given, and the patient improved. Two months later, he developed sudden bilateral lower limb weakness and pins and needles sensation below the waist.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed diffuse cortical and subcortical white matter T2 hyperintensity involving the hippocampi and parahippocampal gyri bilaterally and cortical signal abnormality in the left superior frontal lobe (Figures 1a and 1b). Two weeks later, an MRI (not shown) showed near complete resolution of the abnormal signal intensities. No abnormalities were seen in the cervicothoracic spine.
 
 MRI whole spine two months later showed extensive increased signal of the central cord spanning from C2 to T8 vertebrae with cord expansion (Figures 2a and 2b), and areas of ring-enhancing foci within the cord at C2 (Figures 2c and d). There were also confluent and continuous lesions involving the medial aspect of the right temporal lobe, optic tract, corticospinal tract and hypothalamus. This extends into the right cerebral peduncle and the pyramids of the right pons (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old child presented with a pricking type of upper abdominal pain on and off for 6 months. No history of fever, vomiting, or loss of appetite. No previous history of similar complaints. On examination, tenderness is present over the epigastric and left lumbar region. Routine laboratory investigations were normal.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound, a relatively well-defined heteroechoic lesion with a hyperechoic rim is noted in the retropancreatic area, with a focal exophytic hypoechoic area in the anterior aspect of the lesion (Figure 1). No obvious internal vascularity on colour Doppler.
 
 On contrast-enhanced (CE) CT, a ~ 6.8 x 7 x 8.1 cm well-defined, smoothly marginated, heterodense soft tissue density lesion (predominantly fat component in the posterior aspect with few small solid components and calcific focus in the anterior aspect) is noted in the retropancreatic region (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Regarding relations and mass effect, the lesion, superiorly, abuts the coeliac trunk and inferiorly displaces the third part of the duodenum. Anteriorly, it displaces the body and tail of the pancreas, abuts the splenic artery and mildly compresses the splenic vein. Posteriorly, abuts the left renal vein. Medially, there is a widening of the duodenal C loop, and laterally, a displacement of adjacent bowel loops. There is no obvious evidence of infiltration with the structures mentioned above or vascular complications (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal distension and vague pelvic discomfort. Physical examination revealed a palpable midline abdominal mass. Routine laboratory investigations were unremarkable. Abdominopelvic CT was performed for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Unenhanced abdominopelvic CT demonstrated two large, well-defined, predominantly cystic pelvic masses (Figure 1).
 
 The right-sided lesion (22 × 15 × 19 cm), centred in the cul-de-sac and extending into the right iliac fossa, was intraperitoneal, displacing small-bowel loops superiorly and the uterus postero-leftward while preserving a fat plane with the right psoas muscle (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The left-sided mass (15 × 17 × 15 cm) occupied the left flank/iliac fossa, had a slightly irregular thick wall, and abutted the left psoas muscle, kidney, aorta and iliac vessels without an intervening fat plane, displacing ileo-jejunal loops anteromedially—features consistent with a retroperitoneal origin (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Compression of the left ureter produced moderate pelvicalyceal and proximal ureteral dilatation (Figure 4). A morphologically normal left ovary was identified caudal to the mass (Figure 5), whereas the right ovary was obscured. No ascites, peritoneal implants or pathological lymph nodes were present.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old male presented with headache and giddiness for 3–4 months. He previously experienced diplopia for one month, which resolved following a right-sided medium-pressure ventriculoperitoneal shunt surgery 1 year ago.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed a predominantly cystic lesion with minimal solid components, measuring approximately 24 × 33 × 73 mm, centred in the lower fourth ventricle. It appeared T1 hypointense (Figure 3a) and heterogeneously T2 hyperintense, with multiple fluid-intensity cystic areas (Figure 1a). The solid areas demonstrated minimal post-contrast enhancement (Figure 3b), while the cystic portions remained non-enhancing. Mild diffusion restriction on diffusion weighted imaging–apparent diffusion coefficient (DWI–ADC) sequences (Figures 4a and 4b), and blooming foci were noted on fast field echo sequences, with corresponding PHASE hyperintensities, suggesting haemosiderin deposition (Figures 5a and 5b). CT screening showed tiny calcific foci.
 
 The lesion caused anterior displacement of the posterior pons (Figure 1a), medulla, and upper cervical cord, and abutted the cerebellar hemispheres, vermis, and both middle and inferior cerebellar peduncles. Posteroinferiorly, it extended into the cisterna magna via the foramen of Magendie and into the spinal canal up to 28 mm below McRae’s line. Lateral extension into both cerebellopontine angles was noted via the foramina of Luschka, with associated mild upstream hydrocephalus (Figures 1b and 2a).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 43-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with acute cholecystitis. During imaging work-up, an incidental iliopsoas mass was detected on CT. MRI was subsequently performed for further characterisation.
 Imaging Findings: CT shows a well-defined, complex iliopsoas mass containing fat and soft-tissue components, demonstrating heterogeneous enhancement and prominent internal vessels (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 MRI reveals an encapsulated lesion with high T1 signal intensity, slightly lower than subcutaneous fat, and heterogeneously high T2 signal intensity, with only partial fat suppression (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 2e, and 2f). It exhibits relatively homogeneous hypervascular enhancement with curvilinear branching septations, with no restricted diffusion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 23-year-old woman presented with two weeks of abdominal distension, pelvic pain and urinary frequency. Emergency thoraco-abdominal computed tomography (CT) was performed, followed shortly by dedicated pelvic MRI for loco-regional characterisation.
 Imaging Findings: Initial contrast-enhanced pelvic CT (Figures 1a and 1b) demonstrated a 17 × 16 × 12 cm, predominantly solid, left retro-uterine mass with heterogeneous enhancement on the left ovary, anterior displacement of the uterus, and compression of both distal ureters, resulting in moderate bilateral hydroureteronephrosis. No macroscopic fat, calcification or pathological lymph-node enlargement was identified.
 
 Subsequent pelvic MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) showed mainly solid tissue with intermediate T2 signal (Figures 2a and 2b) and avid post-gadolinium enhancement (Figure 3), containing scattered cystic-necrotic foci. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) showed marked hyperintensity of the solid components with low ADC values, consistent with high cellularity (Figures 4a and 4b). The tumour–uterus interface was smooth, and the right ovary appeared normal, displaced anteriorly. Ascites was minimal and confined to the pouch of Douglas.
 
 No peritoneal implants or pathological lymphadenopathy were identified on either CT or MRI.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 23-year-old woman presented with two weeks of abdominal distension, pelvic pain and urinary frequency. Emergency thoraco-abdominal computed tomography (CT) was performed, followed shortly by dedicated pelvic MRI for loco-regional characterisation.
 Imaging Findings: Initial contrast-enhanced pelvic CT demonstrated a 17 × 16 × 12 cm, predominantly solid, left retro-uterine mass with heterogeneous enhancement on the left ovary, anterior displacement of the uterus, and compression of both distal ureters, resulting in moderate bilateral hydroureteronephrosis. No macroscopic fat, calcification or pathological lymph-node enlargement was identified. Subsequent pelvic MRI showed mainly solid tissue with intermediate T2 signal and avid post-gadolinium enhancement, containing scattered cystic-necrotic foci. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) showed marked hyperintensity of the solid components with low ADC values, consistent with high cellularity. The tumour–uterus interface was smooth, and the right ovary appeared normal, displaced anteriorly. Ascites was minimal and confined to the pouch of Douglas. No peritoneal implants or pathological lymphadenopathy were identified on either CT or MRI.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dysgerminoma, Yolk sac tumour, Juvenile granulosa cell tumour, Mixed malignant ovarian germ cell tumour, Sertoli–Leydig cell tumour, Immature teratoma
FinalDiagnosis: Mixed malignant ovarian germ cell tumour

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old premenarche girl presented with a 3-week history of intermittent hypogastric pain, not being able to sleep for more than two hours at a time for the last 5 days. Clinical examination revealed a palpable pelvic mass and suprapubic tenderness. The patient had no other relevant findings on physical examination. Blood and urine tests were all within normal limits.
 Imaging Findings: A transabdominal pelvic ultrasound was performed and revealed a significant distension of the vagina, measuring 6.8 x 8.3 x 15.3 cm, with echogenic material occupying its lumen (approximately 450 ml). The distension extended to the uterus, which showed hyperechogenic material inside its lumen. Both annexes were of normal appearance. There was no evidence of acute appendicitis, hydronephrosis or other significant findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously healthy 3-year-old boy presented with recurrent atraumatic pain and refusal to walk for several weeks. The right proximal lower leg was swollen, and mobilisation was painful. Laboratory analysis at admission showed increased white blood cell count of 12.6 x 10³/mm³ and C-reactive protein of 20.2 mg/l.
 Imaging Findings: Radiography showed an irregularly moth-eaten mixed osteolytic-sclerotic lesion with a wide zone of transition in the proximal tibial diaphysis. The cortex was permeated with a transverse pathological fracture, interrupted lamellar periosteal reaction, and soft tissue swelling (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed extensive bone marrow replacement in the proximal tibia with adjacent soft tissue mass showing heterogeneous contrast enhancement, cortical permeation, bone destruction with focal cortical breakthrough (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d). Bone marrow changes were hypointense on T1-weighted images (WI) and heterogeneous hyperintense on STIR and fat-suppressed PD images.
 
 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography–computed tomography (FDG PET–CT) scan showed markedly increased uptake in the proximal right tibia, adjacent soft tissue, and right popliteal, inguinal, iliac and retroperitoneal lymph nodes (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Other distant metastases were absent.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 66-year-old woman developed progressive neck pain, headache, and ascending lower-limb paraesthesia over 3 months that culminated in sudden bilateral leg weakness and ataxia. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) showed lymphocytic pleocytosis with hyperproteinorrachia, and a 2024 mediastinal node biopsy revealed non-necrotising granulomas. Her history includes uterine ablation (2006) and sinus surgery (1995).
 Imaging Findings: Sagittal T2-weighted MRI of the spine (Figure 1) revealed diffuse hyperintensity and swelling from C2 to T10 with a sharp transition at T11. Post-gadolinium T1 images (Figure 2) showed confluent dorsal subpial and central canal enhancement, producing the axial “trident sign”. There was patchy nodular enhancement involving the posterior and circumferential pial surfaces. The conus was spared.
 
 Brain MRI demonstrated two punctate cortical–subcortical enhancing foci without mass effect (Figures 3a and 3b). CT chest (Figures 4a and 4b) confirmed bulky right paratracheal and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Compared with prior studies (December 2020), cord swelling and enhancement were mildly reduced after steroid therapy.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old patient with a history of type 2 diabetes, arterial hypertension, and knee osteoarthritis treated with intra-articular steroids was admitted due to fever of unknown origin. The patient reported lumbar pain for the past week, with no associated symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast and contrast-enhanced CT of the skull, thorax and abdomen was performed to identify a possible infectious focus. The key findings included heterogeneous density in both psoas muscles due to the presence of gas bubbles, with no collections detected following contrast administration (Figures 1a and 1b). In addition to the gas identified in the psoas muscles, gas bubbles were observed within the L3 vertebral body.
 
 Unenhanced and contrast-enhanced lumbar spine MRI was performed. The axial T2-weighted image (Figure 2a) shows an enlarged left psoas muscle due to multiple round collections with increased signal intensity. A similar collection is observed in the ipsilateral paraspinal muscle and the right psoas muscle. In the axial post-contrast T1-weighted sequence (Figure 2b), the described lesions, which exhibit enhancement after contrast administration, are also seen (marked with blue stars). Sagittal T1-weighted images pre (Figure 2c) and post-contrast (Figure 2d) demonstrate heterogeneous very subtle enhancement of the L2, L3, L4, and L5 vertebral bodies, as well as the intervertebral discs. The sagittal views demonstrate lesions in the psoas and paraspinal muscles, with hyperintense signal on T2-weighted (Figure 2e) and STIR (Figure 2f) sequences at the level of the L2, L3, L4, and L5 vertebral bodies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with long-standing recurrent epistaxis and progressive fatigue. Physical examination revealed mucocutaneous telangiectasias on the nasal mucosa and tongue, as well as mild hepatomegaly. Laboratory tests showed iron-deficiency anaemia and mildly altered liver function tests, with cholestasis but no hyperbilirubinemia.
 Imaging Findings: Colour-Doppler abdominal ultrasound revealed multiple dilated arterial vessels in both liver lobes, corresponding to branches of the hepatic artery, with one vessel forming a direct communication with the main portal vein (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Subsequent contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated diffuse dilatation of intrahepatic arterial branches and early filling of the dilated hepatic veins during arterial phase, suggestive of arteriovenous shunting, and multiple small hypervascular nodules consistent with hepatic telangiectasias. Mild intrahepatic biliary duct dilatation was also noted, likely due to extrinsic compression by dilated vessels (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Further evaluation with MRI confirmed the presence of intrahepatic arteriovenous malformations. Post-contrast T1-weighted Dixon sequences and MR angiography provided superior characterisation of the vascular anomalies, ruling out portal vein thrombosis and parenchymal changes typical of chronic liver disease (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old patient undergoes a scheduled MRI study due to a one-year history of migraine-like headaches, accompanied by visual disturbances, paraesthesias in the right upper limb, and instability before and during the pain.
 Imaging Findings: The MRI reveals a lesion located in the right temporal horn of the lateral ventricle, with a prominent cystic component and a solid nodule that appears hypointense on T1-weighted images (Figures 1a and 1b) and hyperintense on T2-weighted images (Figure 2). This solid, heterogeneous nodule shows intense enhancement following gadolinium administration (Figures 1c, 1d, and 1e) and areas of microcystic architecture, without evidence of subependymal spread. Extensive T2-FLAIR hyperintensity is observed throughout the surrounding white matter, consistent with vasogenic oedema. The mass exerts a moderate compressive effect on the ventricular system, causing a slight midline shift (Figure 2).
 
 Notably, the lesion displays prominent draining veins, reflecting its hypervascular nature (Figures 3a and 3b). No diffusion restriction is identified on DWI sequences (Figure 4). Perfusion imaging confirms a highly vascularised tumour (Figure 5a, 5b, 5c).
 
 Magnetic resonance spectroscopy demonstrates a prominent lipid and lactate peak, likely indicative of necrotic components (Figure 6).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old lady presented with a gradually progressive, painless, cystic swelling in the left infra-auricular region for the last 12 months. No other swellings were noted in the neck. There was no prior history of ear discharge or parotitis. Clinical examination showed no evidence of skin changes or facial nerve palsy.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography reveals a cystic lesion with internal echoes within the substance of the left parotid gland with a beak-like projection on its deeper aspect (Figure 1).
 
 CT scan demonstrates a well-defined, homogeneous, smoothly marginated, non-enhancing lesion (density of 35 HU before and 36 HU after contrast administration) located in the left parotid gland, causing thinning and outward displacement of the residual gland parenchyma (Figure 2).
 
 MRI reveals a T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, and STIR hyperintense, intra-parotid lesion occupying superficial and deep lobes of the parotid with a small blind-ending tract projecting anteromedially into the parapharyngeal space. No fistulous tracts to the external auditory canal were noted. There was no evidence of fat stranding or enlarged lymph nodes (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old male presented with complaints of headaches for 6 months. No history of loss of consciousness, projectile vomiting or seizures. No history of trauma. The patient is a known case of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension on treatment for 10 years. On examination, macrognathia and frontal bossing were noted.
 Imaging Findings: The skull radiograph (Figure 1a) shows an enlarged sella turcica, frontal sinus hyperpneumatisation, and calvarial hyperostosis. Prognathism and widened mandibular angle are seen (Figure 1b). Hand and feet radiographs (Figures 2a and 2b) show spade-like distal phalanges with prominent ungual tufts. The chest radiograph (Figure 3) reveals widened glenohumeral joints and thickened scapular borders.
 
 CT brain shows calvarial hyperostosis (Figure 4a) and a hyperdense sellar mass (Figure 4b).
 
 MRI reveals a lobulated sellar-suprasellar lesion—T1 isointense (Figure 7), T2 and FLAIR hyperintense with “snowman” appearance—causing sella widening and displacing adjacent vessels (Figures 5a, 5b, and 6), with heterogeneous enhancement (Figures 9b and 9c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 61-year-old male, with a history of a pontine stroke 10 weeks before, presented with new neurological symptoms of staccato speech and an uncoordinated gait. Neurological examination revealed nystagmus, mild dysarthria, truncal and limb ataxia, and an unsteady gait. An MRI was performed to rule out a new ischemic stroke.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI showed a cavitated lesion in the left paramedian pons, hypointense on T1-weighted images (WI) (Figure 1a), hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 1b), without diffusion restriction (Figure 1c), consistent with the previous pontine stroke. Additionally, symmetrical ovoid lesions in the middle cerebellar peduncles (MCPs), hypointense on T1WI (Figure 1d) and hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 1e), and mild T2WI hyperintensity in the left anterior pyramid (Figure 1g), without diffusion restriction (Figures 1f and 1h), were also noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old man presented to the Emergency Department with a 4-day history of right iliac fossa pain. He denied fever, nausea, or vomiting. Physical examination revealed rebound tenderness. Laboratory tests showed elevated C-reactive protein (19.6 mg/L) with a normal white-cell count. Two months earlier, he had undergone a laparoscopic appendectomy.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (Figure 1) revealed a blind-ending, aperistaltic, non-compressible tubular structure (luminal diameter ≈ 10 mm) arising from the caecum at the appendectomy bed. The wall was thickened with surrounding echogenic fat. A minimal amount of free fluid was also observed.
 
 In contrast-enhanced computer tomography (CT), multiplanar reformats demonstrated an enhancing tubular remnant arising from the caecum, capped by two metallic clips at its distal end (Figures 2a, 2b, and 3). The stump showed concentric mural thickening, pericaecal fat stranding, and a trace of free fluid. No evidence of abscess or pneumoperitoneum was identified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 8-year-old male patient presented with chronic left lumbar discomfort, claudication, anaemia (Hb = 8 g/dL), and hyperleukocytosis (12000/mm3). He has been receiving therapy for anaemia for two years.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (US) shows multiple thick-walled collections with echogenic fluid content occupying the left renal fossa and loss of renal parenchyma’s architecture with no corticomedullary differentiation. The thin remaining peripheric parenchymal bundle shows no limits with the thick-walled collections. There is a central pyelic stone measuring 20 mm with a posterior acoustic shadow and a hyperechoic aspect of the perirenal fat.
 
 CT (computed tomography) shows left renal enlargement with destruction of parenchymal architecture and dilated calyces consistent with the “bear paw sign” (Figure 1a), coexisting blurring of perirenal fat, psoas muscle enlargement and abscess formation (Figure 1b), as well as nephroureterolithiasis (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). The left kidney was nonfunctional at the excretory phase scan (Figures 1e and 1f).
 
 On renal scintigraphy (Figure 2), DTPA injection demonstrates reduced relative renal function on the left (17.7% left and 82.3% right), with pyelocaliceal stasis at the excretory phase. Reduced cortical uptake, particularly in the left upper pole, was seen both at DMSA and DTPA scans.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old male presented to the emergency department following the onset of acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain, later diagnosed as inflamed, nonperforated appendicitis. On CT abdomen and pelvis (CTAP), an incidental adrenal mass was found on the left kidney. Work-up for the adrenal mass was conducted.
 Imaging Findings: In 2018, a CTAP noted a 17 mm left adrenal nodule without normal washout characteristics (Figure 1). The work-up at the time revealed no functional secretion, and the patient was also asymptomatic.
 
 A new CTAP revealed that the previous nodule had grown to a size of 6.4 x 6 x 6.7 cm (Figure 2). A chest CT with contrast was unremarkable.
 
 An MRI was ordered for further characterisation of the mass. T1 MRI visualised a heterogeneous enhancing left adrenal mass that was suspicious of malignancy (Figure 3). The size of the lesion was consistent compared to the CTAP.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old healthy male presents with three months of left shoulder pain during activities, followed by a gradual restriction in arm abduction. He denies any trauma, fever, or involvement of other joints. Physical examination reveals swelling in the deltoid region and an inability to raise the left arm to 90°.
 Imaging Findings: Left shoulder x-ray (Figure 1) revealed a mixed sclerotic-lytic lesion involving the proximal diaphysis and metaphysis of the humerus, characterised by poorly defined margins, a wide transition zone, permeative osteolysis, cortical thinning, and a spiculated periosteal reaction with Codman’s triangle, consistent with a Bone-RADS 4 lesion.
 
 MRI demonstrated a large lobulated mass centred in the proximal humerus, hypointense on T1-weighted images (Figure 2a), hyperintense on T2 (Figure 2b) and STIR (Figures 2c and 2d) sequences, with associated extraosseous soft tissue extension and glenohumeral joint effusion. Post-contrast fat-suppressed T1-weighted imaging (Figure 2e) showed heterogeneous enhancement with central non-enhancing areas. Diffusion-weighted imaging (Figure 2f) revealed restricted diffusion with corresponding low signal on the ADC map (Figure 2g) indicating high cellularity.
 
 PET/CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) confirmed an intraosseous mass with osteoid matrix and aggressive periosteal reaction, demonstrating hypermetabolic activity in the proximal humerus, adjacent soft tissues, and secondary foci in the lungs and spinal canal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 83-year-old gentleman presented to the Urosurgery department with a history of lower urinary tract symptoms in the form of increased frequency and incomplete evacuation of urine. No history of haematuria, burning micturition or fever. On digital rectal examination, the prostate gland was felt to be enlarged with nodularity within felt. On biochemical evaluation, prostate specific antigen (PSA) was marginally raised.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound examination, an enlarged prostate with a volume of 36 cc. There were features of chronic bladder outlet obstruction seen in the form of thickening of the bladder wall (maximum thickness of 8 mm) and a few trabeculations in the bladder wall.
 
 In view of the elevated PSA levels, an MRI prostate was done, which revealed a well-defined, solid, irregularly shaped lesion measuring 3.5 x 5.4 x 5.7 cm (AP x TR x CC) arising from the prostatic urethra and causing its widening and extending into the bladder. This lesion was homogeneously hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted and T2-weighted fat-saturated images (Figures 1 and 2). It was showing homogeneous restriction of diffusion on DWI (Figures 3a and 3b) and was heterogeneously enhancing on post-contrast images. Also, multiple well-defined T2 heterogeneous nodules were seen in the transitional zone (Figure 4). No T2 hypointense or diffusion-restricting lesion was seen in the prostate.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 74-year-old female undergoing immunosuppressive therapy was referred to the orthopaedic department for evaluation of a progressively enlarging mass on the medial side of the left knee, evolving over six months. Initially asymptomatic, the lesion became painful. Notably, she had undergone an articular puncture one year earlier.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with a large mass on the medial aspect of the left knee (Figures 1a and 1b), which was also evident on plain radiography (Figure 2).
 
 Ultrasonography demonstrated a sizable extra-articular heterogeneous lesion with no internal vascularisation, accompanied by a moderate joint effusion and synovial hypertrophy (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed a large extra-articular collection, hyperintense on proton density-weighted fat-suppressed sequences (Figures 4a and 4b) and hypointense on T1-weighted sequences (Figure 4c), with extensive peripheral enhancement following gadolinium administration (Figures 5a and 5b) and no enhancement of solid components. Additionally, MRI revealed extensive synovitis and bone marrow oedema, along with juxta-articular and intra-articular erosions exhibiting peripheral contrast enhancement. No blooming artefacts were identified (Figure 5c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male presented with headache, giddiness, disorientation, and right lower limb weakness. He had a recent history of a fall.
 Imaging Findings: Initial MRI revealed a crescent-shaped extra-axial lesion along the right frontoparietotemporal convexity, hyperintense on T1 and FLAIR-weighted sequences (Figures 1a and 1b), with mild mass effect and midline shift, suggesting a subacute subdural haematoma. A similar extra-axial collection was seen on the left side. No diffusion restriction was observed on DWI (Figure 1c). A well-defined enhancing lesion was also seen in the right lateral ventricle (persistent in serial imaging).
 
 The patient was initially managed conservatively and scheduled for follow-up. Two months later, a non-contrast CT was performed due to recurrence of symptoms, including disorientation and right lower limb weakness. CT revealed a well-defined subdural extraxial crescent-shaped collection along the cerebral convexity in the left frontoparietal region. It was causing a mass effect on the underlying cerebral parenchyma. Midline shift was noted towards the right, with left uncal herniation and dilatation of the contralateral lateral ventricle. A heterogeneous predominantly iso to hypodense lesion was seen along the left frontoparietal convexity, causing mass effect on the underlying brain parenchyma and left lateral ventricle (Figure 2a). Mild periosteal reaction along the inner table of the left parietal bone was observed (Figure 2b).
 
 No update MRI with gadolinium contrast was performed between this CT and surgery. This may have limited diagnostic clarity and contributed to incomplete preoperative planning.
 
 Intraoperatively, no haematoma was identified. Instead, thickened dura, granular periosteum and hyperostosis of underlying bone were noted, raising concern for an underlying neoplastic process (Figure 3).
 
 Postoperative contrast-enhanced MRI revealed a well-defined extra-axial lesion in the left frontoparietotemporal region. It was hyperintense on T2/FLAIR, isointense on T1, with mild diffusion restriction and heterogeneous peripheral enhancement (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c and 4d). There was also a significant mass effect on the adjacent brain parenchyma and ventricles. T1 hyperintense and T2 hypointense areas likely represented postoperative changes. A thin underlying T2 hyperintense, T1 hypointense, and non-enhancing collection were also noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old male presented with worsening lower back pain for 6 months. On physical examination there was no deformity or neurological deficits noted. There was no history of fever, night sweats or weight loss.
 Imaging Findings: An anteroposterior (AP) and lateral radiograph (Figures 1a and 1b) of the lumbar spine was done initially. It showed reduced intervertebral disc space at the L2–L3 level with adjacent end plate erosions and sclerosis, and soft tissue opacity in the paravertebral region at the L2–L3 lumbar vertebral level. Loss of the psoas muscle outline was also noted.
 
 On MRI, partial destruction of the posteroinferior aspect of L1 and posterosuperior aspect of L2 is seen with involvement of the intervening IV disc (Figures 2a and 2b). Altered signal intensity is seen in the vertebral bodies of L4 and L5, which appear hypointense on T1-weighted (T1WI) and hyperintense on T2-weighted (T2WI). There is an associated extensive, multiloculated prevertebral and bilateral paravertebral collection seen (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). The soft tissue components appear hypointense on T1WI and hyperintense on T2W images. These show peripheral restriction of diffusion (Figures 7a and 7b). On post-contrast imaging (Figures 5a, 5b, and 6), there is enhancement of the soft tissue components with central necrotic areas and the involved parts of L1 and L2 vertebral bodies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old female presented to the emergency department with acute lower abdominal pain, accompanied by chronic burning sensations and stabbing pain in the right lower leg. Based on the initial clinical presentation, diverticulitis was suspected, and a routine abdominal CT scan was performed for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: CT imaging demonstrated a lipomatous, highly vascularised mass located posterior to the proximal femoral diaphysis of the right hip, with extension into the ischiofemoral space (Figures 1a and 1b). The lesion was predominantly composed of fatty tissue and exhibited intense arterial enhancement, consistent with a hypervascular process.
 
 Subsequent MRI offered further characterisation, identifying a complex intramuscular mass within the right quadratus femoris muscle, composed of both lipomatous (hyperintense on T1-weighted images) and nodular components (Figure 2). The nodular areas appeared hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2 fat-suppressed sequences (Figure 3). These regions demonstrated moderate, heterogeneous contrast enhancement, indicating variable internal vascularity (Figure 4). There were no significant signal voids on T2* sequences, effectively excluding haemorrhage or calcification. The lesion did not invade surrounding structures. However, the adjacent sciatic nerve exhibited focal thickening, which was suggestive of possible irritation due to mass effect or local inflammation (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 15-year-old female patient with right lower limb shortening since childhood was evaluated in consultation for limb lengthening surgery. Her personal history was negative for trauma. On physical examination, she had a clinically measured leg-length discrepancy of 4 cm.
 Imaging Findings: Full-length standing anteroposterior view radiograph (Figure 1a) demonstrated right lower limb shortening secondary to femoral hypoplasia, with normal ossification and no proximal deformity, consistent with Paley type 1A congenital femoral deficiency (CFD). Mild proximal fibular shortening was also noted, compatible with fibular hemimelia (Achterman–Kalamchi type IA).
 
 On the magnified AP radiograph of the right knee (Figure 1b), the femoral intercondylar notch appeared shallow, and the tibial spines were markedly hypoplastic.
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur (anterior drawer sign) and uncovering of the lateral meniscus posterior horn (Figure 2b), findings suggestive of anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) insufficiency. On mid-sagittal fat-suppressed proton-density (PD) and oblique sagittal PD-weighted images (Figures 2c and 2d), the ACL was not visualised. The posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) was present and vertically oriented (positive PCL line sign). No bone marrow oedema was seen, and the menisci were unremarkable.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 57-year-old diabetic man, with 25 years of bilateral lower limb weakness, presented with a 1.5-month history of a mildly painful, firm, non-tender swelling over the left shoulder. Limited shoulder movement, reduced sensation, and a scar noted (Figures 1a and 1b). No trauma or aspiration records.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the left shoulder (Figure 2) showed joint destruction, including humeral head and glenoid resorption, widened joint space, periarticular calcifications, and soft tissue inflammation.
 
 MRI of the left shoulder (Figures 4, 5, 6, 7a, and 7b) revealed extensive bony damage, complete tears in the rotator cuff tendons and complete destruction of the glenoid labrum, glenohumeral ligaments, and biceps-labral anchor. A large, thick-walled fluid collection with peripheral enhancement extended into the intermuscular and intramuscular planes of the rotator cuff muscles.
 
 MRI of the spine (Figures 3a and 3b) showed a syrinx extending from the cervico-medullary junction to the thoracic vertebra at T11, consistent with syringomyelia, and a wedge collapse of the vertebral body of T12 thoracic vertebra, indicating chronic vertebral involvement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old male presented with severe triventricular ventriculomegaly and cortical thinning at 35 weeks on prenatal ultrasound. At birth, he had an unusual phenotype, adducted thumbs and poor spontaneous mobility. Progressive ventriculomegaly required ventriculoperitoneal derivation (VPD). Currently, the patient has severe neurological involvement, flaccid paresis with paroxysms of rigidity and epilepsy.
 Imaging Findings: Transfontanelar US and postnatal MRI at 1 day and 1 week, respectively, show severe triventricular hydrocephaly and large massa intermedia (Figures 1a and 1b), aqueductal stenosis, enlarged quadrigeminal plate, corpus callosum agenesis, vermian hypoplasia, severe cortical thinning and diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d).
 
 The latest MRI, at the age of 3 years, before VPD treatment and mild improvement of ventriculomegaly, demonstrates better visualisation of the previous findings (Figure 3a), in addition to diffuse hypoplasia of cerebral white matter, polymicrogyria (Figures 3b and 3c), diencephalosynapsis (Figure 3d) with diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia in a so-called “butterfly-like pattern” (Figures 3e and 3f), and hypoplastic and poorly differentiated hippocampi.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male with epigastric pain primarily localised to the right upper quadrant, a positive Murphy’s sign, associated with vomiting and hypotension. The patient presented with a fever spike of 38.1ºC as the only infectious clinical sign observed. Among the analytical parameters, there is a marked elevation of C-reactive protein (CRP; 12.8 mg/dL), procalcitonin (29.8 ng/mL), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH; 2488 U/L), and leukocytosis (24×10^9/L) with neutrophilia (18.9×10^9/L). The family reports diarrhoea in the preceding days.
 Imaging Findings: In a portable chest x-ray, a small left pleural effusion and the presence of gas in the right upper quadrant are seen (Figure 1).
 
 An abdominopelvic CT scan was performed with intravenous contrast in the arterial and portal venous phases. The main findings include numerous subcentimeter gas bubbles arranged in two clusters, primarily affecting the right liver lobe, with maximum axial diameters of 80 mm and 69 mm. Free gas bubbles within the parenchyma and perfusion abnormalities in the affected segments are also observed, along with predominantly central periportal oedema (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 22-year-old female presented with hallux valgus deformity of both first toes since birth (Figure 1). At the age of 12, she began experiencing difficulty walking and progressive joint stiffness. On physical examination, there was an inability to fully open the temporomandibular joints bilaterally and restricted flexion and abduction of the extremities.
 Imaging Findings: On plain radiographs of the feet, there was absence of the distal phalanges of the first toes and hallux valgus with metatarsophalangeal angles >15°, supporting early skeletal deformities (Figure 2). CT imaging of the cervicothoracic spine demonstrated heterotopic ossification of the supraspinous and posterior nuchal ligaments (Figure 3). Three-dimensional reconstructions showed multiple bridging ossifications forming tubular bone between the iliac bones and the twelfth ribs, as well as ossified extensions from the right iliac bone to the right humerus (Figures 4a and 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old patient with no notable personal or family medical history. The patient has been complaining for the past 3 years of bilateral gonalgia, right shoulder pain, and rachialgia, with more significant functional discomfort in the lower limbs. Clinical examination reveals tenderness upon palpation of the knees and right shoulder.
 Imaging Findings: A radiological assessment was performed (x-rays and full-body scan) revealing multiple exostotic lesions of the dorsolumbar spine, sternoclavicular joints, costal arches, scapulas, as well as in bones of the upper limbs (bilaterally, more marked at the metaphyseal-epiphyseal region of the right proximal humerus), pelvic girdle, and bones of the lower limbs and feet (bilaterally, more marked at the knees).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old male cricket player presented with anterior knee pain for 3 months, aggravated by jumping and stair climbing. No history of trauma or systemic symptoms. Localised tenderness was noted at the inferior patella without swelling or effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A skyline Merchant radiograph of the right knee (Figure 1) showed irregular ossification and subtle fragmentation at the inferior pole of the patella, with a normal tibial tuberosity. Sagittal and axial musculoskeletal ultrasound images (Figures 2a and 2b) revealed cortical irregularity and fragmentation of the inferior patellar pole. The patellar tendon was intact. A sagittal ultrasound image (Figure 3) also revealed a hyperechoic, mildly prominent Hoffa’s fat pad, suggestive of reactive inflammatory changes.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 5-year-old girl presented with a progressively enlarging occipital swelling and mild pain. She had prior right-eye surgeries for eyelid coloboma, with corneal opacification of uncertain cause. She had no light perception in the right eye and reduced vision in the left. No systemic issues. Siblings were healthy.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT revealed a 2.8 cm bony defect at the posterior fontanelle, through which a partially calcified lipomatous lesion was herniating into the subcutaneous scalp (Figure 1a). This lesion extended intracranially along the posterior falx, causing indentation of the superior sagittal sinus (Figure 1b). A second linear lipoma (10 × 2 mm) was seen at the splenium of the corpus callosum (Figure 1c). A 3D volume-rendered reconstruction of the cranial defect shows the 2.8 cm bony defect at the posterior fontanelle (Figure 1d).
 
 MRI confirmed T1-hyperintense lipomas at the posterior fontanelle and splenium, with an extracranial component (Figure 2a). T2-weighted MRI revealed an arachnoid cyst at the left cerebellopontine angle (Figure 2b), and confirmed bilateral colobomas and absence of the right lens (Figure 2c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old male presented to the emergency department at our institution with the chief complaints of insidious, throbbing frontal headache, dizziness, photo-phonophobia and emesis for one week. Neurological examination revealed a positive Kernig sign, ataxia and impaired finger-to-nose testing.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head demonstrated a diffuse, heterogeneous solid-cystic lesion in the right inferomedial cerebellar hemisphere involving the adjacent vermis. The solid component was fairly defined with irregular margins, manifesting as mild expansion of the cerebellar folia with abnormal low T1 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) and high T2 FLAIR signal interspersed with thick T2 dark striations (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). The lobulated cystic component showed high signal on T2WI, intermediate on T1WI and incomplete FLAIR suppression without diffusion restriction.
 
 Post-contrast images demonstrated nodular and linear enhancement within the folia and peripheral enhancement of the cyst (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). Mass effect in the posterior fossa included mild perilesional oedema and partial effacement of the fourth ventricle. There was no evident enlargement of supratentorial ventricles or periventricular oedema. We observed an inferior cerebellar tonsillar descent of 7 mm.
 
 CT showed thick, linear striated calcifications mimicking a “tiger-striped” appearance. Bony remodelling with scalloping of the overlying occipital bone suggested an indolent long-standing lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head demonstrated a diffuse, heterogeneous solid-cystic lesion in the right inferomedial cerebellar hemisphere involving the adjacent vermis. The solid component was fairly defined with irregular margins, manifesting as mild expansion of the cerebellar folia with abnormal low T1 and high T2 FLAIR signal interspersed with thick T2 dark striations. The lobulated cystic component showed high signal on T2WI, intermediate on T1WI and incomplete FLAIR suppression without diffusion restriction. Post-contrast images demonstrated nodular and linear enhancement within the folia and peripheral enhancement of the cyst. Mass effect in the posterior fossa included mild perilesional oedema and partial effacement of the fourth ventricle. CT showed thick, linear striated calcifications mimicking a “tiger-striped” appearance. Bony remodelling with scalloping of the overlying occipital bone suggested an indolent long-standing lesion.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma, Pilocytic astrocytoma, Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma, MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion), Oligodendroglioma-like tumour
FinalDiagnosis: Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma, MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously healthy 45-year-old gentleman presented in the neurology department with a 6-month history of neurocognitive decline with behavioural and personality changes associated with myoclonus and gait imbalance.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain demonstrated asymmetrical areas of high signal in DWI and T2/FLAIR with corresponding normal to low signal in ADC along the cortical convexities of the right frontoparieto-temporal lobes and the right parasagittal region (Figures 1a, 1b, 2a, and 2b). Bilateral symmetric high signal in DWI and T2/FLAIR with corresponding low signal in ADC are seen in the bilateral caudate nucleus, globus pallidus and bilateral posteromedial thalamus (Figures 3a, 3b, and 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 28-year-old pregnant female presented to the neurology department with new-onset generalised tonic-clonic seizures. Neurological examination was normal. A dermatological consultation revealed multiple acneiform scars over the flexor surface and pearly papules along the eyelid margins and on the forehead (Figures 1 and 2), for which she had been under evaluation. No family history of similar illness was noted. MRI brain was performed to investigate the aetiology of seizures.
 Imaging Findings: Clinical photographs taken show acneiform scars over the flexor aspect of the left elbow (Figure 1) and pearly papules along both eyelid margins, and also seen on the forehead (Figure 2).
 
 An MRI brain was done. Axial T2 image (Figure 3) shows bilateral symmetrical hypointensities in the amygdala. There is no mass effect, oedema, or acute infarct. There is no space-occupying lesion. Axial SWI (susceptibility-weighted imaging) of the MRI brain (Figure 4) sequences shows blooming in the amygdala, likely due to calcification.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old woman taking etanercept for psoriatic arthritis presented to the emergency department with pain in her right wrist for three weeks, predominantly in the fourth and fifth fingers. She reported no history of trauma or prior procedures. Clinical examination revealed swelling on the palmar surface and pain with movement. An ultrasound was performed, which showed synovial fluid (images not available).
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the right hand showed fluid in the synovial sheath of the flexor tendons (Figure 1a), extending from the distal radioulnar joint to the metacarpals, with multiple small nodules within the tendon sheath, ranging from hypointense to isointense, consistent with “rice bodies” (Figures 1b, 1c, and 1d). Soft tissue enlargement and thickening of the flexor sheath tendons were also observed (Figures 1e and 1f).
 
 Two months after starting treatment, a follow-up MRI was performed, which showed the disappearance of the “rice bodies” with a slight persistence of synovial fluid in the flexor tendon sheath (Figures 2a and 2b). A decrease in soft tissue enlargement and flexor sheath thickening was also observed (Figure 2c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 35-year-old gentleman presented with a gradually progressive headache since 6 months, which was eventually accompanied by visual disturbance, giddiness and difficulty in walking. There was no history of trauma or any other systemic complaints. Fundoscopic examination was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced MRI brain revealed a 3.1 x 3.4 x 3.5 cm-sized T2 hypointense (Figure 1), and thick-walled, ring-enhancing (Figure 2) lesion in the left hemicerebellum. The lesion showed T1 isointense signal (Figure 3) without any diffusion restriction (Figures 4a and 4b). Associated, there was significant perilesional oedema and mass effect causing compression of the fourth ventricle (Figure 1) with resultant moderate upstream hydrocephalus and periventricular cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) seepage. On MR spectroscopy, the lesion showed a prominent lipid lactate peak with reduced choline and N-acetylaspartate levels (Figures 5a and 5b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 An 8-year-old boy presented with recurrent focal seizures since infancy and progressive developmental delay, along with a port-wine stain on the right side of his face, present since birth.
 
 Case 2
 
 A 54-year-old male with a right hemifacial port-wine stain presented with long-standing epilepsy, progressive left-hemiplegia, cognitive decline and migraine-like headaches. Ophthalmologic evaluation revealed visual field defects and glaucoma.
 Imaging Findings: Case 1
 
 Non-contrast CT images of the brain revealed gyriform, curvilinear cortical and subcortical calcifications along the right parieto-occipital region, with a “tram-track” appearance and associated mild cortical atrophy (Figure 1a). Post-contrast CT images demonstrated pial enhancement over the right parieto-occipital cortex, consistent with pial angiomas. There was also enlargement and enhancement of the ipsilateral choroid plexus (Figure 1b). Additionally, homogeneous crescentic enhancement of the choroid was found along the posterior segment of the right globe, consistent with a choroidal angioma (Figure 1c).
 
 Case 2
 
 T2-weighted and FLAIR axial MR images show right cerebral volume loss with diffusely thinned cortex, ex vacuo dilatation of ipsilateral lateral ventricle, and extensive curvilinear cortical hypointensity (Figures 2a and 2b). Axial susceptibility-weighted image (SWI) shows “blooming” of the extensive cortical/subcortical calcifications on the right cerebral hemisphere (Figure 2c). Post-contrast T1-weighted image shows serpentine gyriform enhancement, and enlargement and enhancement of choroid plexus (Figure 2d).
PostDescription:

 Case 2: T2-weighted and FLAIR axial MR images show right cerebral volume loss with diffusely thinned cortex, ex vacuo dilatation of the ipsilateral lateral ventricle, and extensive curvilinear cortical hypointensity. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 : A 9-year-old girl presented to the otorhinolaryngology (ENT) department with profound hearing loss. On evaluation, she was detected to have right-sided sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL).
 
 Case 2
 
 : A 6-year-old boy was referred from the ENT department with a clinical diagnosis of severe right-sided SNHL.
 
 Case 3
 
 : A 2-year-old child was referred with a clinical diagnosis of right-sided SNHL for MRI.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain were performed, and the most relevant findings for each case are noted below:
 
 Case 1
 
 : Thinned-out cochlear division of the right vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII) (Figure 1b). Stenosed right internal auditory meatus (IAM) (Figure 1a). The facial nerve CN VII appeared normal.
 
 Case 2
 
 : Non-visualised cochlear division of the right CN VIII (Figure 2a). CN VII appeared normal.
 
 Case 3
 
 : The cochlear nerve is not visualised in the anteroinferior aspect of the right IAM (Figure 3a).
